h: Holding the bass (this is technique, not comfort trivia)
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 0 · Start Here Gear & setup Ten minutes of naming parts and adjusting straps saves months of fighting your own instrument. None of this requires spending money. The parts that matter Tuning machines Nut Frets (dots = 3 · 5 · 7 · 9 · 12) Pickups Bridge Volume & tone Thickest string (E) at the top of this view — when the bass hangs on you, that string is closest to the ceiling. Tuning machines — the pegs you'll meet on the next page. Tighter = higher. Nut and frets — the string vibrates between the bridge and either the nut (open) or the fret you press. Pickups — magnetic microphones under the strings. Pluck over different pickups and listen: near the neck = round and deep, near the bridge = tight and growly. Volume & tone knobs — day-one settings: volume up, tone around the middle. That's genuinely all you need for now. Holding the bass (this is technique, not comfort trivia) Strap height: adjust so the bass sits in the same place standing or sitting — bridge roughly at your belt, neck angled slightly up (30–40°). Too low looks cool and wrecks your fretting wrist; too high cramps your plucking arm. Sitting: body of the bass on your right thigh (if right-handed), and still use the strap — the left hand should never be holding the neck up , only playing it. Fretting hand: thumb pad on the back of the neck, roughly opposite your middle finger, like holding a peach you don't want to bruise. Fingers curl and press just behind the fret, not on top of it, with the fingertip. Plucking hand: rest your thumb on a pickup, and pluck with the pads of your index and middle fingers — alternating. (A pick is legitimate too; this course defaults to fingers.) Both shoulders down. Check every few minutes at first; tension you practice becomes tension you perform. The honest gear list A working bass, a cable, anything that makes it audible (amp, headphone amp, or audio interface), and ideally a strap and a tuner (this site has one). Nothing else. New strings and a professional setu
You can get from 'just picked it up' to 'ready to play, relaxed' in ten seconds, standing or sitting.
You can hear the difference between plucking near the neck and near the bridge, and between tone knob up and down.

h: How to match a pitch by ear
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 0 · Start Here Tuning up An out-of-tune bass makes everything you play sound wrong — including the things you play correctly. Thirty seconds of tuning buys you an hour of honest feedback. The idea Your four strings are named for the notes they play when open : E, A, D, G , from the thickest to the thinnest. Tuning means turning each string's machine head (the pegs on the headstock) until the string's pitch matches its reference note. Turning the peg tightens or loosens the string: tighter = higher , looser = lower. Small turns — an out-of-tune string is usually only a hair away. Hear your references E thickest · 41.2 Hz A 55.0 Hz D 73.4 Hz G thinnest · 98.0 Hz ▶ Play all four Hold the tone (drone) Tap a pad, then play your matching open string and adjust until the two sounds melt into one. With "hold the tone" on, the reference sustains while you tune — listen for the slow wobble (beating) between the two sounds; it gets slower as you get closer, and vanishes when you're in tune. How to match a pitch by ear Play the reference and your string at the same time . If you can't tell higher from lower: tune your string clearly down until it's obviously too low, then come up slowly. Near the target you'll hear a slow wah-wah-wah wobble — that's the two pitches interfering. Slower wobble = closer. No wobble = in tune. Always finish by tuning up to the note — it keeps the string from slipping flat while you play. The details (open as much as you like) The 5th-fret cross-check Once one string is right, the bass can check itself: the 5th fret of each string sounds the same note as the next open string . Hold E-string fret 5 (that's an A) and compare it to your open A string; then A-string fret 5 against open D; then D-string fret 5 against open G. The second practice exercise below walks it. Why basses drift out of tune Strings are steel under a lot of tension: temperature swings, fresh strings still stretching, enthusiastic playing, and bumping the pegs all shift them. C

t: Practice: Match each open string to its reference
b: Each open string rings against its reference tone with no wobble — four strings, under a minute.
Each fretted note and the open string above it sound identical — if one pair wobbles, you know exactly which string to fix.

t: Welcome — how to use this site
b: Unit 0 · Start Here Welcome — how to use this site This is a bass course that runs entirely in your browser: fretboards you can tap, notation that plays itself, a metronome, ear training, and a path that goes from "which one is the E string?" to reading real charts. What this site believes Three things, and everything else follows from them: 1. Plain words first. Every idea starts in everyday language, and the official term arrives only after you already understand the thing it names. When we say "notes that are two frets apart", you'll learn later that musicians call that a whole step — and by then it's just a new name for something you already know. Any term with a dotted underline is tappable and will explain itself. 2. Your ears and hands come along. Music theory that you can't hear is trivia. Every example on this site is playable — tap a fret diagram and it sounds, press ▶ under a line of notation and it plays. And every page ends with something concrete to practice on a real bass, with a starting tempo and a target. 3. Small, honest steps. Each page teaches one idea, checks it with a short quiz (asked in plain words, not vocabulary), and tells you where it leads. Quiz questions you miss turn into flashcards automatically, so the things you personally find hard come back around. How a page works Every topic page has the same flow: The idea — a plain-English explanation, usually with an everyday analogy. Hear it / find it — an interactive diagram or playable notation. Sound always starts with your tap — nothing plays on its own. The details — collapsible layers. Open as much as you like; you can follow the whole course without ever opening the deepest layer. Practice — exercises with notation and tab , a ▶ button so you know what they should sound like, tempo targets, and a "log practice" button that remembers what you've worked on. A quiz — a few questions to let you claim the page. Finishing the quiz marks the page complete on the concept map; it never blocks

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 1 · Reading Music Rhythm & counting Wrong note, right time: the band barely flinches. Right note, wrong time: everyone hears it. For a bassist, rhythm literacy IS literacy. Before you start Nothing but a steady foot. Notes' names were last page; this page is entirely about when . The idea Time in music is a grid. The beat is the steady pulse; a bar boxes up (usually) four of them. Written rhythm just says how many boxes each note fills: a ♩ quarter note fills one beat, a ♪ eighth note half a beat, a half note two, a whole note four. Silence is written too — rests fill boxes with deliberate quiet. And the trade secret: musicians don't feel rhythm as math, they feel it as syllables . Quarters: "1, 2, 3, 4". Eighths: "1 & 2 & 3 & 4 &" (say "and" for &). Count out loud — it feels silly for exactly two days, and then you can read rhythm. Hear the grid Toggle boxes on the eighth-note grid, press play, and count along with the clicks — the accented click is beat 1. how fast the beat goes (tempo) 80 ▶ Play the grid Quarters All eighths Something funkier The grid is one bar of 4/4 sliced into eighths: cells read "1 & 2 & 3 & 4 &". Count out loud while it plays — the counting is the skill. The values, in one table Note Length in 4/4 Count it 𝅝 4 beats (a whole bar) "1 — (2, 3, 4 held)" 𝅗𝅥 2 beats "1 — (2 held)" ♩ 1 beat "1" ♪ ½ beat "1" or "&" 𝅘𝅥𝅯 ¼ beat "1", "e", "&", or "a" 𝄽 1 beat of silence count it, don't play it The details (open as much as you like) Dots and ties — the length modifiers A dot after a note adds half its own length: dotted quarter = 1½ beats ("1 & 2", landing again on "& of 2"). A tie gluing two notes means pluck once, hold through both — it's how a note reaches across a bar line. Dotted-quarter + eighth is the rhythm of thousands of bass lines; you'll drill it below. Rests are a bass superpower Reggae, funk, and Motown lines are famous for the holes in them. A rest means stop the string — with the fretting hand relaxing or the plucking hand

t: Practice: Quarters and rests, spoken and played
b: Notes land exactly on the click, rests are exactly silent (string stopped, not fading), and your counting never pauses.

t: Practice: Eighth notes with the '&' spoken
b: A full bar of eighths sits evenly inside the beat — no rushing the '&'s — at 80 bpm with alternating fingers.
The second note lands cleanly on the '& of 2' and '& of 4' — the lopsided-on-purpose feel that powers half of pop bass.

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 1 · Reading Music The staff & bass clef Written music is a graph: time runs left to right, pitch runs bottom to top. Learn five lines and one fancy F, and the graph starts talking. Before you start Just the note-name alphabet (A to G, then repeat) — covered in Unit 0 . You do not need to read music already; that's what this unit is for. The idea The notes live on a grid of five lines called the staff. Higher on the grid = higher pitch ; each line and each space between lines is one letter name. But five anonymous lines could start anywhere — so music plants a flag: the 𝄢 bass clef wraps its two dots around the second line from the top, declaring this line is F . From that one anchor, every other line and space follows alphabetically. In plain English The clef is the "you are here" marker. Bass clef: fourth line up = F. Count lines and spaces up or down the alphabet from there. The lines and spaces Bottom to top, the lines are G–B–D–F–A ( Good Boys Deserve Fame Always ) and the spaces are A–C–E–G ( All Cows Eat Grass ). Press play and follow along: ▶ Play the lines ▶ Play the spaces Where your strings live Notes that spill off the grid get mini extension lines — ledger lines … no wait, those are called ledger lines (tab comes two pages from now). Your open E string sits on the first ledger line below the staff; the other three open strings climb from there: ▶ Play E · A · D · G One honest small print: bass parts are written an octave higher than they sound — otherwise almost everything would drown in ledger lines below the staff. Every bass player on Earth reads this way; you never have to think about it again. The details (open as much as you like) How to actually learn the notes (not just the mnemonic) Mnemonics bootstrap; landmarks make you fluent. Burn in three anchors: F on the clef's own line , A at the top line , and middle-of-the-staff D . Read neighbors as "one above A", "the space under D" — exactly like you learned the fretboard by landmarks, and muc

t: Practice: Lines: see it, say it, play it
b: You name each line note out loud a beat before you play it, in time, at 60 bpm.

t: Practice: First real reading: lines and spaces mixed
b: Eight mixed notes read cold — no mnemonics recited, just landmarks — cleanly at 60 bpm.

h: How the two systems divide the truth
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 1 · Reading Music Tab ↔ notation Tab tells your fingers where . Notation tells your ears what and when . This site shows both, stacked — here's how to read the pair as one thing. Before you start The staff ( previous-but-one page ) and counting ( previous page ). The idea Tab is a picture of your bass: four lines = four strings , with the low E string at the bottom — exactly like the fretboard diagrams you've been tapping. A number on a line means "press that fret on that string"; 0 means open. It's instantly readable… and it's silent about rhythm. Plain tab can't say whether a note lasts two beats or half of one. That's why this site (like most good bass books) stacks tab under real notation: read the rhythm from the top staff, take the fingering from the bottom one. Same music, two lenses. Same phrase, both lenses ▶ Play the phrase Follow the top staff for the rhythm you learned last page; glance down for which fret. After a while the two reads merge into one. How the two systems divide the truth Question Notation answers Tab answers Which pitch? yes — exactly indirectly (you'd have to know the fretboard) How long? What rhythm? yes — completely no (unless rhythm stems are added) Which fret/string to use? no — your choice yes — exactly Works for any instrument? yes no — it IS the bass diagram Sight-read new music? the standard for it slow without the recording The details (open as much as you like) Why the same note appears at different frets Remember from the fretboard unit (or discover now): most pitches live in several places — the D at A-string fret 5 is also D-string fret 0. Notation leaves the choice to you; tab records one specific choice. When this site's tab picks a position, it's optimizing for staying in one hand position — feel free to disagree once you know why. Internet tab, honestly Text tab from the internet is wonderful and wrong: fingerings are often awkward, rhythms absent, and errors common. Use it as a sketch — then let the recording be th
You can play the page's phrase three ways: from the stacked pair, from tab alone (recalling the rhythm), and from notation alone (choosing your own frets).
You can play D–E–G twice — once using open strings, once fully fretted at the 5th position — and hear that pitch is identical while feel differs.

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 1 · Reading Music Time signatures The two stacked numbers are a promise about how time is boxed. Most music you'll play keeps one promise — 4/4 — but the others feel wonderful in the body, and bassists are the ones who make them feel right. Before you start Counting quarters and eighths out loud ( rhythm & counting ). The idea A time signature like 4/4 says: top number — how many beats per bar ; bottom number — which note value counts as one beat (4 = quarter note, 8 = eighth note). So 4/4 is four quarter-note beats per bar; 3/4 is three. But the signature also implies a stress pattern : 4/4 leans ONE-two-THREE-four (with 1 the strongest); 3/4 spins ONE-two-three; 6/8 rolls in two big pulses, each made of three little ones — ONE-two-three FOUR-five-six. Same clock speed, totally different dance. Feel the difference 4/4 — march/rock 3/4 — waltz 6/8 — rolling tempo 96 ▶ Start the count The bright first light is beat 1. Count out loud with each meter and sway — seriously, sway. Meter is physical. The signatures you'll actually meet Signature Count Where it lives 4/4 1 2 3 4 Rock, pop, funk, blues, jazz — ~90% of everything. Also written 𝄴. 3/4 1 2 3 Waltzes, country ballads, "My Favorite Things". 6/8 ONE-2-3 FOUR-5-6 Slow blues, doo-wop ballads, Irish jigs, "House of the Rising Sun". 2/4 1 2 Marches, polkas, fast country — feel of "oom-pah". 12/8 four rolling 3s Slow blues at full grandeur — 4/4 where every beat is a triplet. The details (open as much as you like) 6/8 vs 3/4 — the classic confusion, settled Both contain six eighth notes' worth of time, but they group differently: 3/4 is three beats subdivided in twos (ONE-and TWO-and THREE-and); 6/8 is two beats subdivided in threes (ONE-two-three FOUR-five-six). Your foot taps 3 times per bar in 3/4 and 2 times per bar in 6/8. When in doubt, find the foot. What the bassist does about all this The bass states the meter more than any other instrument: landing the root on beat 1 is what tells everyone's body where

t: Practice: Waltz bass: own beat 1 in 3/4
b: Beat 1 lands like furniture being set down — solid, unhurried — while 2 and 3 stay light, for a full minute without drifting.

t: Practice: 6/8: play the two big pulses
b: You sound only the two dotted-quarter pulses per bar while counting all six eighths out loud — the rolling feel appears by itself.

h: The five (plus one) shapes that matter first
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 2 · The Fretboard Intervals as shapes Here's the fretboard's best secret: a distance between two notes is a picture your hand can hold — and the picture works from any starting note, anywhere on the neck. Before you start Note names on the neck ( previous page ) and the word interval = distance between two notes. The idea Because the strings are tuned evenly (five frets apart, every pair), a given musical distance always makes the same visual shape . A fifth is always "one string up, two frets over" — whether you start on E, on B♭, at the 3rd fret or the 10th. Learn five shapes and you can build most of the bass lines in this course without hunting for names first. See them, hear them Fifth Fourth Octave Major third Minor third Minor seventh Pick a shape — then tap any fret on the E or A string to move the root there. The shape follows. The five (plus one) shapes that matter first Interval Shape from the root Sounds like / used for Fifth (7 semitones) up 1 string, up 2 frets power, stability — the root's best friend in every style Fourth (5 semitones) up 1 string, same fret the next-string note; smooth motion between roots Octave (12) up 2 strings, up 2 frets the same note, higher — disco/funk jumps Major third (4) up 1 string, back 1 fret the "happy" color note of major chords Minor third (3) up 1 string, back 2 frets (or same string +3) the "sad" color note of minor chords Minor seventh (10) up 2 strings, same fret the bluesy tension note of 7th chords Down-a-fifth deserves a mention too: same fret, one string down lands a fifth below the root — the "low fifth" country and rock lines bounce off constantly. The details (open as much as you like) Why shapes beat letter names (at first) Names tell you what a note is; shapes tell your hand where relationships live . Real bass lines are built from relationships — "root, fifth, octave, flat-seven" — so a movable shape vocabulary means one lick learned = twelve keys owned. The names still matter (you'll call shapes

t: Practice: Root–fifth, walked up the neck
b: The fifth shape lands without looking, from four different roots, both as up-a-fifth and down-a-fifth (same fret, string below).

t: Practice: Major vs minor third: hear the color flip
b: From the same root, you can play both thirds back-to-back and hear (and name) happy vs. sad — from any root you pick.
Root–octave–root bounces cleanly (octave = 2 strings up, 2 frets over) with the octave popping out evenly, disco-style, at 90 bpm.

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 2 · The Fretboard Notes on the fretboard The neck looks like a hundred anonymous places to put your fingers. It's actually a small, repeating map — and you only need a few landmarks to never feel lost on it again. Before you start You should be in tune ( tuning page ) and know that your strings, low to high, are E–A–D–G. The idea Music uses just twelve note names , then they repeat. Seven of them are the plain letters A–G, called the natural notes ; the other five live between them and are named with ♯ (one fret up) or ♭ (one fret down). On a bass, the map is dead simple: each fret is one semitone . Start on any open string and walk up one fret at a time and you pass through all twelve names; at the 12th fret you're back where you started, one octave higher. That's why the 12th fret has a double dot — it's the "you've gone all the way around" marker. Find it Show the C's All natural notes An octave pair Clear Tap any fret to hear it and see its name. Sound starts with your first tap. Notice how every C sits in the same visual relationship to its neighbors — shapes repeat everywhere. The landmarks Don't memorize 48 positions. Learn a few anchors and derive the rest: Open strings: E, A, D, G. Free notes, no fingers. Fret 3 on the A string is C. The single most useful note to know — huge amounts of music live around it. Fret 5 replays the next open string. E-string fret 5 is A; A-string fret 5 is D; D-string fret 5 is G. (That's also how you cross-check your tuning.) Fret 12 = the open string, an octave up. Double dot, same name. The dots at 3 · 5 · 7 · 9 · 12 are your GPS — with them you never have to count frets from the nut. The octave shape The single most useful shape on the bass: from any note, go two strings up and two frets over and you land on the same note an octave higher. Press "An octave pair" above to see one. This shape turns every note you learn into two notes — learn the E and A strings well and the octave shape hands you the D and G strings for f

t: Practice: Natural notes up the E string
b: You can play E–F–G–A–B–C–D–E up the low string, naming each note out loud, without pausing to count frets.

t: Practice: Natural notes up the A string
b: Same drill on the A string — and you can find C (fret 3) instantly, eyes closed.

t: Practice: Octave shapes across the neck
b: From any note on the E or A string, your hand finds the octave (two strings up, two frets over) without looking.

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 2 · The Fretboard Positions & fingering Your four fingers can own four neighboring frets without moving your hand. Decide once which finger owns which fret, and most "where do I put my fingers?" questions answer themselves forever. Before you start Interval shapes ( previous page ) — positions are where shapes live. The idea Park your hand so your index finger hovers over, say, fret 5. Then: index = fret 5, middle = 6, ring = 7, pinky = 8 . That claim — one finger per fret — defines a position (this one is "5th position", named after the index finger's fret). Within a position you can reach every note in a 4-fret × 4-string window — around 25 notes — without your hand traveling an inch. See the window 1st position 3rd position 5th position 7th position Numbers show which finger owns each fret: 1 = index … 4 = pinky. Tap any marked fret to hear it. The rules of thumb (literally) Thumb behind the neck , roughly opposite your middle finger, pointing up-ish. It's a pivot, not a clamp. Fingers stay curled and close to the strings — hovering a centimeter away, not flying up between notes. Press with fingertips just behind the fret , as you learned in setup — the position system tells you which tip. Near the nut, relax the rule : frets 1–4 are wide, so many players cover them index-middle-pinky (1-2-4, the "three-fret" fingering borrowed from upright bass). Both systems are correct; big stretches are not. Shifting: moving house gracefully Shifts — moving the whole hand to a new position — are planned, not panicked. The pros' tricks: shift during an open string or a rest; lighten the thumb and glide (don't hop); and lead with the finger that will play the next note, arriving just as it's needed. The last exercise below drills exactly this. The details (open as much as you like) Why bother with a system at all? Because hesitation is the enemy of time. When every note in the window has exactly one finger assigned, your hands stop making decisions mid-bar — and decision-f
All four fingers place notes of equal volume and length across all four strings in 3rd position — no flams, no buzzes — at 70 bpm.
The shift from 3rd to 5th position happens during the long note — silently, calmly — and the landing note speaks exactly on the beat.

h: Fact 1 — the strings are all a fourth apart
Fact 2 — fret 5 duplicates the next string
Fact 3 — every fret multiplies by the same tiny amount
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 2 · The Fretboard Strings & fret math Four strings, tuned a fixed distance apart, sliced by frets into equal steps. Three facts — and the entire neck becomes a system instead of a mystery. Before you start Your strings are in tune ( tuning ) and you know one fret = one semitone . Fact 1 — the strings are all a fourth apart E → A → D → G: each string sits exactly five semitones (five frets' worth) above its thicker neighbor. That regular spacing is a gift — every pattern you learn works across ALL string pairs, because the relationship between neighbors never changes. (Guitarists aren't so lucky; their B string breaks the pattern.) Fact 2 — fret 5 duplicates the next string Direct consequence: press fret 5 and you've recreated the next open string. Tap around and prove it: E5 = open A A5 = open D D5 = open G All three pairs Tap the buttons — or any fret — to hear it. Same note, two homes. This is why the same lick can be played in several places, and why your tuning cross-check works. Fact 3 — every fret multiplies by the same tiny amount Each fret raises the string's vibration speed by about 6% (precisely: ×2 1/12 ≈ 1.0595). Do that twelve times and you've exactly doubled the frequency — which your ear hears as "the same note, higher": the octave at fret 12. Equal steps are also why the frets get physically narrower up the neck: each one cuts off 6% of the remaining string length. In plain English Frets are a ruler marked in equal musical steps. Twelve steps = one octave = double the vibration speed. The narrowing spacing isn't decoration — it's the 6% rule made visible. The details (open as much as you like) Why tune in fourths at all? Five frets is what a hand can comfortably span. Tuned this way, one hand position reaches a two-octave patchwork of notes without moving — wider tuning would mean stretches, narrower would mean constant shifting. Fourths are the ergonomic sweet spot, which is why the bass, the double bass before it, and most of the guitar all se

h: The progressions you already know by heart
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 3 · Theory Core Diatonic harmony — a key's chord family Why do certain chords "belong together"? Because a key manufactures its own chords — seven of them, with fixed personalities and numbered name tags. Learn the tags and you can hear a song once and know what the bass should do. Before you start Scale degrees , triads , and seventh qualities . The idea Build a triad (1-3-5 stack) on each degree of a major scale, using only the scale's own notes — diatonic means exactly that. The spacing of the scale forces each chord's quality; nobody chooses it: I major · ii minor · iii minor · IV major · V major · vi minor · vii diminished Written as Roman numerals (uppercase = major, lowercase = minor, ° = diminished), the pattern is the same in every key — which is why musicians say "a I–V–vi–IV " instead of naming four chords twelve different ways. Session players' Nashville numbers are the same idea with digits: 1 5 6− 4. Every key's family, generated Show sevenths ▶ Hear the family (I→vii°) Degree Roman Nashville Chord Notes Tap a row's ▶ to hear that chord with its root under it — the bassist's-eye view of harmony. The progressions you already know by heart Numerals In C You've heard it in… I–IV–V C–F–G the blues, early rock & roll, punk — the primary colors I–V–vi–IV C–G–Am–F a comical fraction of all pop hits since 1990 ii–V–I Dm7–G7–Cmaj7 jazz's handshake; the strongest resolution in the language I–vi–IV–V C–Am–F–G 1950s doo-wop, "Stand By Me" vi–IV–I–V Am–F–C–G the moody rotation of the pop wheel The details (open as much as you like) Why V pulls home so hard The V chord contains the key's leading tone (degree 7, a semitone under home) — and as a V7 it adds the 4, forming a tritone that must resolve. Both tension notes collapse inward onto the I chord. Your root motion V→I underlines the strongest gravity harmony has; that's why cadences live or die by the bass. Minor keys run the same factory Natural minor generates: i · ii° · III · iv · v · VI · VII. Same machi

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 3 · Theory Core Intervals — distances with personalities You already play interval shapes. This page attaches the proper names and — much more useful — the sounds , so "a fifth" stops being geometry and becomes something you recognize with your eyes closed. Before you start Interval shapes on the neck ( Unit 2 ) and counting frets as semitones . The idea Every interval has a two-part name: a number (how many letter names you cross, counting both ends: C→E crosses C, D, E = a third ) and a quality (major, minor, perfect — the fine-tuning). The everyday translation: Semitones Name Short Sounds like the start of… 1 minor second m2 Jaws (tension itself) 2 major second M2 Happy Birthday ("hap-py") 3 minor third m3 Smoke on the Water riff; "sad" chord color 4 major third M3 Oh When the Saints; "happy" chord color 5 perfect fourth P4 Here Comes the Bride; your next open string 6 tritone TT The Simpsons ("the SIMP-sons"); maximum itch 7 perfect fifth P5 Star Wars theme; power and stability 8 minor sixth m6 The Entertainer's opening lean 9 major sixth M6 My Way ("and now…"); the NBC chime 10 minor seventh m7 Star Trek (original) theme; bluesy pull 11 major seventh M7 Take On Me's leap; dreamy ache 12 octave P8 Somewhere Over the Rainbow ("some-WHERE") Hear them One after the other Both at once 🎲 Random root Pick an interval — it plays from the current root and draws itself on the neck. The details (open as much as you like) Why "perfect"? Why "major/minor"? Unisons, fourths, fifths, and octaves are so acoustically stable that old theorists called them perfect — they don't come in happy/sad flavors. Seconds, thirds, sixths, and sevenths do: the larger version is major , one semitone smaller is minor . That's the entire naming system. (Shrink a perfect interval and it's diminished ; stretch it, augmented — the tritone is both an augmented fourth and a diminished fifth, which suits its troublemaker reputation.) Consonance and dissonance — the working bassist's version Oct
You can sing the fifth or octave above a played root before you play it — and the check note proves you right, 8 times out of 10.

h: Reading a key signature at a glance
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 3 · Theory Core Keys & the circle of fifths "What key is it in?" is the first question at every jam. The answer names the home note, the seven-note palette, and — via one elegant circle — how every key relates to every other. Before you start The major scale and its recipe; the fifth as an interval. The idea A key is a home base plus its major (or minor) scale: "in G major" means G feels like home and the music draws from G's seven notes. Rather than re-flagging F♯ on every line, written music declares it once — the key signature after the clef. Now the beautiful part. Walk from C up a perfect fifth to G, and G's scale differs from C's by exactly one note (F becomes F♯). Up another fifth to D: one more sharp. Every fifth you climb adds one sharp; every fifth you descend adds one flat. Twelve steps of this and you're back home — a closed circle . The circle, live Tap any key — outer ring majors, inner ring their relative minors. It plays the scale and shows the signature. Neighbors on the circle share six of seven notes — that's why songs drift to them so easily, and why the two chords next to your key (IV and V!) feel like family. Reading a key signature at a glance Sharps: the last sharp is the leading tone — go one semitone up from it and you've named the key. (Two sharps, last is C♯ → key of D.) Flats: the second-to-last flat IS the key. (Three flats B♭-E♭-A♭ → E♭ major.) With one flat, memorize it: F. The sharps always arrive in the order F-C-G-D-A-E-B; flats in the exact reverse. Nothing to compute — it's the circle again. The details (open as much as you like) What the circle does for a bassist, concretely Three jobs: (1) Instant key-signature lookup. (2) The notes either side of your key are its IV and V — the two most common chords in the language, pre-located. (3) Root motion by fifths (C→F→B♭…) is the strongest sound in harmony; countless progressions and the entire ii–V–I move counter-clockwise along this circle. Practicing anything "around the circl
You can play the circle's roots in order — C G D A E B F♯ D♭ A♭ E♭ B♭ F — two beats each, without consulting the diagram.

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 3 · Theory Core The major scale — music's measuring stick Do–re–mi is not a children's song, it's the coordinate system. Chords, keys, other scales, even "wrong notes" are all described by how they differ from this one seven-note recipe. Before you start Whole and half steps ( two frets / one fret ) and interval names . The idea The major scale is a recipe of steps: Whole, Whole, Half, Whole, Whole, Whole, Half — in frets: 2 · 2 · 1 · 2 · 2 · 2 · 1 . Start on any note, apply the recipe, and you get that note's major scale. Start on C and it happens to use only natural notes (C D E F G A B); start anywhere else and sharps or flats appear because the recipe demands them , not by decoration. Each note gets a number — its degree : 1 2 3 4 5 6 7, then 1 again (the octave). Those numbers are how musicians talk: "root and 5", "walk up to the 3", "flat 7". Learn the numbers with your hands now and every future page gets easier. One shape, every key Because the recipe is fixed, the fretboard shape is fixed too. Here's the classic one-octave box (starting finger 2 on the root): G major A major C major E♭ major ▶ Play the scale Show degrees Show names Same picture, different address — that's the whole trick. Written out (G major) ▶ Play Notice the key signature: one sharp (F♯). That's the recipe showing up in writing — next page explains the bookkeeping. The details (open as much as you like) Why those two half steps matter The half steps fall between degrees 3–4 and 7–8. Degree 7 sits one semitone under home and leans into it (play 7 then stop — feel the itch). Degree 4 leans down onto 3. These two gravitational spots are why melodies resolve where they do — and why the approach notes you'll use in bass lines work. Degrees have names too 1 tonic (home) · 2 supertonic · 3 mediant · 4 subdominant · 5 dominant (the other pole) · 6 submediant · 7 leading tone (the leaner). Bassists mostly say the numbers — but "tonic" and "dominant" appear on every theory page ever written,

h: Hear the flip, see the shape
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 3 · Theory Core Minor scales Most of the music you love that isn't sunshine lives here. The good news: you already own the notes — minor is the major scale wearing different gravity. Before you start The major scale and relative keys (the circle's inner ring). The idea Take C major's seven notes but make A feel like home instead of C, and everything changes character: same palette, darker painting. That's the natural minor scale — and every minor scale is some major scale's relative , rooted three semitones below it (A minor ↔ C major, E minor ↔ G major…). Measured from its own root, natural minor's recipe is 2 · 1 · 2 · 2 · 1 · 2 · 2 , and in degree language it's the major scale with ♭3, ♭6, ♭7 — three notes shifted one fret down. The ♭3 alone flips the mood; you heard exactly this on the thirds page. Hear the flip, see the shape ▶ A natural minor ▶ A major (compare) Root A Root E Root C Same root, two scales — the ♭3 does most of the emotional work. The details (open as much as you like) Relative vs parallel — two ways to "go minor" Relative minor shares notes but moves home (C major ↔ A minor — zero accidentals change). Parallel minor keeps the home and changes notes (C major ↔ C minor — flat the 3rd, 6th, 7th). Charts and the circle think in relatives; your ear at a jam usually notices parallels ("same root, sadder"). Keep both words — they answer different questions. Harmonic & melodic minor (the famous tweaks) Natural minor's ♭7 doesn't lean into home the way major's leading tone does. Classical harmony fixes it by raising the 7th — harmonic minor — creating that instantly-recognizable exotic gap between ♭6 and 7. Raising the 6th as well smooths the melody — melodic minor . For now: recognize the names, know why they exist (stronger pull to the root), and file them for the Expert unit. Rock, pop, and funk live overwhelmingly in natural minor (and its pentatonic — next page). Minor key signatures A minor key borrows its relative major's signature: E minor

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 3 · Theory Core Pentatonics & the blues scale Remove a scale's two riskiest notes and what's left can hardly sound wrong. Five notes power more famous bass lines than the other seven-note theory combined. Before you start Major and minor scales as degree numbers. The idea The minor pentatonic is natural minor minus its two half-step-adjacent notes (the 2 and ♭6): 1 · ♭3 · 4 · 5 · ♭7 . No note sits a semitone from any chord tone, so nothing clashes hard — which is why you can lean on any of these five over a groove and sound intentional. The major pentatonic plays the same trick on the major scale: 1 · 2 · 3 · 5 · 6 (drop the 4 and 7) — instant country, gospel, and Motown sweetness. Add one spicy chromatic note to the minor pentatonic — the ♭5 — and you have the blues scale : 1 · ♭3 · 4 · ♭5 · 5 · ♭7. The ♭5 is a passing sting, not a home: slide through it and it's the blues; sit on it and it's a horror film. The box The famous minor-pentatonic "box" fits one position perfectly — this is probably the most-played hand shape in popular music: Minor pentatonic Major pentatonic Blues (+♭5) ▶ Play it Root A Root E Root G The details (open as much as you like) Why five notes feel so safe (one layer deeper) The notes removed are precisely the ones that form semitone rubs against chord tones: the 4 sits on the 3's shoulder, the 7 leans on the root, minor's 2 and ♭6 crowd the ♭3 and 5. What remains keeps a polite whole-step-or-more distance from trouble. Less tension available — but total permission to move freely. That trade IS the pentatonic sound. Relative pentatonics — one shape, two jobs A minor pentatonic and C major pentatonic contain the identical five notes (relatives again!). So the box you learn today is also your major pentatonic — just anchored on a different root dot. This is the only-learn-it-once economy that makes pentatonics such good value. Bassists vs guitarists, pentatonically Guitarists solo with this scale; bassists build with it. The classic rock/

h: Reading chord symbols — the survival grammar
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 3 · Theory Core Seventh chords & how charts spell them Stack one more third on a triad and harmony grows up: blues, soul, funk, and jazz are written almost entirely in these four-note chords — and in the shorthand this page teaches you to read. Before you start Triads — sevenths are triads plus one decision. The idea A seventh chord adds the 7th degree above the root. Like the third, the seventh comes in two sizes (major 7 = 11 semitones, minor/"flat" 7 = 10) — and the combination of triad + seventh size gives the four workhorses: Chord Build Written Personality Major seven major triad + M7 Cmaj7, CΔ7 dreamy, floating, "jazz ballad" Dominant seven major triad + ♭7 C7 bluesy, restless, wants to move Minor seven minor triad + ♭7 Cm7, C−7 mellow, warm, soul's default Half-diminished dim triad + ♭7 Cø7, Cm7♭5 tense, noir, minor keys' ii Beware the classic trap: C7 and Cmaj7 are different chords. A plain 7 always means the bluesy ♭7; "maj7"/Δ marks the dreamy natural 7. Their sevenths sit one fret apart and one world apart. Hear the four flavors maj7 7 m7 ø7 (m7♭5) ▶ Chord ▶ Arpeggio Root C Root G Root A Reading chord symbols — the survival grammar Letter = root (your note!). Alone = major triad. m / − / min = minor third. maj / Δ before a 7 = major seventh. A number (7, 9, 13) = that degree stacked on; plain numbers imply the ♭7 underneath. ° dim , ø , + aug , sus = the triad modifications you've met. Slash (C/E) = chord left of the slash, bass note right of it — the one symbol that's literally an instruction to you personally. Full symbol table with pronunciations: the symbol reference keeps them all searchable. The details (open as much as you like) What the bassist actually plays over sevenths Roots and fifths still pay the rent. The upgrade sevenths offer is the ♭7 as a melodic hook (the R–♭7 rock/soul move) and the 3rd to signal quality in walking lines. You almost never need to sound all four notes — you imply them, one tasty tone at a time. Unit 5 does exact

h: Build one, hear it, walk it
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 3 · Theory Core Triads — the atoms of harmony Every chord chart you'll ever read is mostly these: three notes, stacked in thirds. The band plays them together; you'll play them apart — that's what a bass line mostly is. Before you start Thirds and fifths as sounds and shapes . The idea A triad = root + third + fifth . The fifth is scaffolding (perfect, stable); the third picks the mood. Mix and match the two third-sizes and two fifth-sizes and you get exactly four flavors: Triad Recipe (semitones) Symbol Character Major root + 4 + 3 (M3 then m3) C bright, settled Minor root + 3 + 4 Cm, C− dark, settled Diminished root + 3 + 3 C°, Cdim clenched, unstable Augmented root + 4 + 4 C+, Caug floating, eerie Played one note at a time, a triad is an arpeggio — and arpeggios are the most chord-proof material a bass line can be made of: you literally cannot clash with the chord while spelling it. Build one, hear it, walk it Major Minor Diminished Augmented ▶ As a chord ▶ As an arpeggio Root G Root A Root C The one-position arpeggio shapes From a root on the E or A string, both essential flavors sit under one hand: major = root (finger 2), third one string up one fret back (1), fifth one string up two frets over (4). Minor = same but the third slides one more fret back. Practice below burns these in — they're the skeleton keys of Unit 5. The details (open as much as you like) Where triads come from (stacking the scale) Take a scale, keep every other note: degrees 1-3-5. Do it starting from each degree and the scale generates seven triads — its private chord family. That factory (and its Roman-numeral naming) is the diatonic harmony page, two stops from here. Why diminished sounds "clenched" Squash the fifth from 7 semitones to 6 and you've built a tritone into the chord — the itchiest interval, demanding resolution. Diminished triads are doors, not rooms: music passes through them. (Augmented stretches the fifth to 8 and floats free of gravity instead — rarer, dreamier.) P

h: Why slow works (it's not about caution)
Chunking: the loop that fixes things
A session shape that works (20–30 min)
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 4 · Technique How to practice The difference between two years of progress and two years of repeating year one is not talent or hours — it's method. This page is the method, and it fits on an index card. The index card The whole method 1. One specific goal per session ("clean shift in bar 3", not "get better"). 2. Slow enough to be perfect; the metronome decides, not your mood. 3. Loop the smallest broken piece, not the whole song. 4. Raise the tempo only after 3 clean repeats; drop back two notches after every miss. 5. Short and daily beats long and weekly. Log it (the buttons on every exercise here do this). Why slow works (it's not about caution) Every repetition — clean or sloppy — is a vote for what your hands will do automatically. Practicing mistakes at speed literally trains the mistakes. Slow practice is how you cast only good votes; speed then arrives on its own, unnervingly fast. The rule of thumb: if you can't play it perfectly three times in a row, it's too fast to be practice. Chunking: the loop that fixes things Never "run it again from the top". Find the two beats that break, loop just those (with one note of lead-in and one of follow-through), fix them, then reattach: bar → phrase → section. Ten minutes of surgical looping beats an hour of hopeful run-throughs — this is the single largest efficiency upgrade available to any self-taught player. A session shape that works (20–30 min) 2 min — tune (always; it's also ear training). 5 min — hands warmup : the 1-2-3-4 permutations or alternation drill, with the metronome. 10–15 min — the one goal : this week's exercise or song chunk, looped and logged. 5 min — music : play something you love, badly or not. This is why you started; guard it. The routine builder in the Practice Labs unit assembles this automatically from your practice log and progress. The details (open as much as you like) Plateaus and what they mean Progress is stairs, not a ramp: weeks of nothing, then a jump — usually right after t
One complete 20-minute session executed to the shape above, with a written one-line goal, and the goal met or honestly assessed.

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 4 · Technique The left hand — clean, light, and legato The fretting hand's job description is shorter than people think: arrive early, press lightly, leave cleanly. Plus two tricks — hammer-ons and pull-offs — that turn effortful lines into flowing ones. Before you start Positions & one-finger-per-fret — this page is about making that system sound good. Pressure: the eye-opener Try this now: fret any note, pluck repeatedly, and slowly release pressure until the note buzzes — then press just enough to clean it up. That's the required force. Most self-taught players use three times more, and pay for it in speed, stamina, and sore hands. Light touch isn't a style; it's physics. Hammer-ons & pull-offs Two notes, one pluck: hammer-on (snap a finger down onto a higher fret — written h in tab) and its mirror the pull-off (flick the finger off sideways so the lower note speaks — written p ). Together with slides , they're how bass lines get that vocal, connected ( legato ) quality — and how fast figures stop costing plucks. The silent skills Finger independence — each finger moves without its neighbors flinching. The permutation drill below is the classic builder. Fretting-hand muting — the underside of your fingers lies gently across higher strings; releasing pressure (without leaving the string) stops notes. Half of funk's crispness is this. Economy of motion — fingers hover a centimeter from the strings. Watch any great player: the hands look lazy. That's the goal. The details (open as much as you like) Where exactly behind the fret? As close behind the metal as comfortable — pressure needed drops dramatically there. In one-finger-per-fret position your index and pinky can't both be perfect; prioritize whichever is playing. "Just behind the fret" beats "middle of the box" every time you can afford it. Hammer/pull mechanics that actually work Hammer-ons are a snap from the knuckle , not a push — speed makes the note, not force. Pull-offs are a tiny sideways pluck by
On three different frets (low, middle, high) you can find the buzz point and settle just above it — and your default grip noticeably lightens over a week.
Four permutation patterns cleanly at 60 bpm, one note per click, every finger landing on its own fret with even volume.
The unplucked note is at least 80% as loud as the plucked one — hammered and pulled, on every string pair you try.

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 4 · Technique The right hand — where your sound is born Ninety percent of "that bassist sounds amazing" is the plucking hand: evenness, note length, and quiet strings. It's built from three boring habits, practiced until they're invisible. Before you start Anchoring and posture from gear & setup . (Left-handed players: mirror everything — "right hand" here means the plucking hand.) The three habits 1 — Strict alternation. Index, middle, index, middle ( fingerstyle ). No favorites, no doubling when nervous. Alternation is what makes fast passages possible later — and it only becomes automatic if it's absolute now, at slow tempos. 2 — The rest stroke. Pluck through the string so your finger lands on the next-thicker string ( rest stroke ). Fat tone and free muting in one motion — the landed finger silences the string it touches. 3 — Everything else stays quiet. The thumb rides the pickup or the E string; idle fingers and the fretting hand's underside damp what isn't speaking. Muting isn't a skill you add later — it IS the technique. Where to pluck You chose a spot on the setup page by ear; now make it a decision per song: near the bridge = tight, defined, cuts through ( tone for busy lines); near the neck = deep, round, huge (great for slow roots); in between = the everyday default. Moving your hand is a free EQ pedal. The details (open as much as you like) How hard to pluck (softer than you think) Digging in constantly costs evenness and speed, and the amp can be loud for you. Practice at a firm-but-relaxed medium and save the dig for accents — dynamics are only available to players who don't live at maximum. A useful check: your plucking fingers should never feel tired before your brain does. Raking — the legal shortcut Descending across strings, let one finger fall through both ( raking ) instead of forcing alternation. G-string then D-string with one finger's sweep is smoother AND quieter than two separate plucks. Alternation is the rule; raking is its offici

t: Practice: Pure alternation on open strings
b: Sixteen even eighth notes per string, i-m-i-m without exception, identical volume from both fingers — recorded and verified by your own ears.
Crossing E→A→D→G and back keeps the i-m alternation unbroken (no sneaky double-index at the crossings) at 80 bpm.
The same four roots played twice: once ringing full value, once cut to half value by plucking-hand touch — cleanly, on time, by choice.

h: The habits that make time solid
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 4 · Technique Timing & groove — the actual job Nobody hires a bassist for fast fingers. They hire the feeling that the floor is solid. This page is how that feeling is built: a metronome, subdivision, and honest listening. Before you start Counting out loud — this page turns counting into feel. The idea Solid time isn't hitting beats like a machine — it's continuously feeling the subdivision so every note lands where you chose, not where your nerves put it. The metronome isn't a judge; it's a drummer who never rushes. Your relationship with it goes through three stages: playing to it, playing with it, and finally hearing it disappear because you're locked ( in the pocket ). Your practice metronome tempo 70 clicks per beat 1 — quarters 2 — eighths 4 — sixteenths ▶ Start Play the exercises below against this. (The full groove machine with chords and styles lives in the Practice Labs unit.) The habits that make time solid Subdivide out loud (or in your chest, later). Long notes drift when nothing is counting through them. Place, don't react. Aim each note at the click; don't wait to hear the click and chase it. Record yourself weekly. Twenty seconds of phone audio tells the truth about rushing that your in-the-moment brain hides. Note length is part of rhythm. Where a note ends grooves as much as where it starts — you built that control on the right-hand page. The details (open as much as you like) The classic drills, explained Half-time click: set the metronome to half your tempo and hear it as beats 2 and 4 (the snare drum) — instantly exposes rushing. Gap practice: mute the click every other bar (our groove machine can); if you're off when it returns, the drift is yours. One-click-per-bar: the endgame — you keep the whole bar's time yourself. Ahead, behind, and "on top" — feel vocabulary Great rhythm sections place notes a hair early (urgent, punk), dead-center (neutral, pop), or a hair late (relaxed, soul) — consistently, by choice . That's "feel". It's only a
Eight whole notes in a row land exactly with the click while you count '1-2-3-4' through each — the hardest easy exercise on this site.
A one-bar root groove stays locked for two minutes with the click only on beats 2 and 4 — and it starts to feel BETTER than four clicks.
Continuous eighths with an accent only on beat 1, then only on the '& of 2' — accents move, the grid doesn't.

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 4 · Technique Tone & dynamics — the sound between the notes Two bassists, same bass, same line: one sounds like a recording, the other like a rehearsal. The difference is rarely gear. It's the five controls that live in your hands. Before you start The right-hand page (pluck position, rest strokes) — this page turns those mechanics into intent. The five hand-controls Control What varies What it's for Pluck position bridge ↔ neck tight growl ↔ deep bloom; pick per song Pluck strength feather ↔ dig dynamics — phrases that breathe; accents that mean something Note length staccato ↔ legato the groove's crispness; reggae vs ballad in one variable Fretting cleanliness buzz ↔ bell pressure + placement from the left-hand page Muting discipline rumble ↔ silence one string speaking at a time — the pro sound in a nutshell Dynamics are relative An accent only pops if everything around it is quieter. Living at maximum volume ("digging in" full-time) doesn't sound powerful — it sounds flat, because nothing can stand out. The paradox of loud: play the non-accents softer and the whole line sounds bigger. f and p in written music are exactly this game, formalized. The amp, briefly and honestly Start flat (EQ knobs at noon), volume comfortable — fix your hands before your EQ. Tone knob on the bass : rolling it down darkens; try it with palm muting for instant Motown. If it sounds bad recorded, suspect (in order): muting, note length consistency, pressure/buzz, THEN gear. The details (open as much as you like) Palm muting — the third voice Rest the edge of your plucking palm on the strings at the bridge and pluck with thumb or fingers: notes become warm thumps with a fast decay — the upright-ish sound of countless Motown and reggae records ( palm mute ). It trades sustain for punch; note-length control comes free because nothing rings anyway. Consistency before color Interesting tone is variation around a controlled center . The exercises below build the center: ten identical not
Ten plucks on one fretted note that are indistinguishable in volume, tone, and length — verified by recording, on two different strings.

t: Practice: Dynamic terraces: p → mf → f → p

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 5 · Bass Lines Approach notes — aiming at the next chord The difference between chords-played-in-a-row and a line is one small habit: spending the last moment of each bar traveling toward the next root. Three ways to travel, one rule about timing. Before you start Chord tones on the beats — this page is about the space between the beats. The idea An approach note is played just before a target (usually the next chord's root), one step away from it, so the landing sounds aimed rather than teleported. The three flavors: Chromatic — one fret above or below the target. Works over any chords, in any key, because it borrows its meaning entirely from where it's going. The pro's pocketknife. Scale (diatonic) — one scale step above or below. Smoother, more "inside" than chromatic. Dominant — the fifth of the target, dropped in just before it. The strongest pull in harmony, miniaturized. The timing rule Approach notes live on weak moments — the last eighth or last beat of the bar. Target lands ON the change. Tension on the weak, resolution on the strong: that's the whole grammar. Hear the difference No approaches Chromatic from below Chromatic from above Dominant approach Same G–C–D–G progression; only the last eighth note of each bar changes. Small habit, different instrument. The details (open as much as you like) Why chromatic approaches never sound wrong A note a semitone from its target resolves so fast the ear files it as motion, not harmony — it's heard as belonging to where it's GOING. That's why F♯ works before G even in keys with no F♯: context is destination. The only way to break a chromatic approach is to be late — then it's just a wrong note standing still. Passing tones: the same idea, mid-bar A passing tone connects two chord tones by step on a weak beat (root … 2 … 3rd). Same grammar as approaches — non-chord notes are fine while moving on weak time. Put one on a strong beat and hold it, and you'll hear why the rule exists. Double chromatic & enclosure (
G–C–D–G with the last eighth of each bar approaching the next root from one fret below — landings inevitable, approach never early.
The same progression with a different approach type each pass (below, above, dominant) — chosen before the bar starts, not improvised in panic.

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 5 · Bass Lines The bassist's job The bass sits exactly where rhythm meets harmony — one hand in the drummer's world, one in the chords. Understand the job description and every note-choice question gets easier. Before you start Diatonic harmony and a steady pocket . The two duties Duty 1 — say when. With the kick drum, you define where the beat lives. People's bodies move to the bass; if you rush, the whole room rushes. Duty 2 — say what. When the chord changes, your note is what tells everyone's ear the harmony moved. Play the root on the change and the band sounds organized. This is why the same guitar chord over a different bass note is a different chord to the listener — you hold the harmony's steering wheel (remember slash chords: C/E is an instruction to you). The whole unit in one sentence Land the right note (usually the root) at the right moment (usually the chord change), keep the pocket, and decorate only in the space that's left. Hear the job happen The same two bars, three ways — pads play the chords, bass underneath: 1 — Roots only 2 — Roots + rhythm 3 — Too busy (on purpose) Play all three. Version 2 is the job. Version 3 is what enthusiasm sounds like before Unit 5. Space is a note choice Listen to any beloved bass line: the holes are composed as carefully as the notes. Rests create the room that makes the next note land; constant sound is wallpaper. The discipline of Unit 4's note-length work becomes, here, an aesthetic: you are allowed to be simple , and the greats usually are. The details (open as much as you like) Locking with the kick drum, concretely "Lock with the drummer" means: know where the kick pattern is, land your low notes with it (or in deliberate dialogue with it), and match note endings to the drums' space. In the groove machine lab you can hear root-cue patterns against the click — practice matching them exactly, then leaving gaps where they leave gaps. When NOT to play the root Slash chords (play what's written), walking line

h: The two moves to steal today
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 5 · Bass Lines Roots & chord tones — the 80% of everything Root, fifth, octave, then the third and seventh: rank a chord's notes by job security and you've ranked most bass lines ever recorded. Here's the hierarchy and how to spend it. Before you start Triads , sevenths , and their shapes . The hierarchy of usefulness Note Job When to spend it Root names the chord the change — beat 1 of a new chord, almost always Fifth stable motion without leaving home beat 3, the "&" before a change, country/rock bounce Octave energy without new information lifts, disco/funk pops, doubling intensity Third announces major/minor mid-bar color; first beat when the chart says so (C/E!) Seventh style and lean the R–♭7 rock/soul hook; walking descents Notice what's NOT on the list yet: everything outside the chord. That's the next page — and it's seasoning, not food. Hear the hierarchy Roots Roots + fifths + octaves + thirds & sevenths Four passes over the same A7–D7 vamp, each adding the next tier. Every version is a real, usable line. The two moves to steal today Root–fifth alternation — the two-beat heartbeat under country, polka, and half of rock. Remember the low fifth trick: same fret, one string down. Root–octave bounce — the energy machine of disco and funk (finger 1 and finger 4, the fixed claw from Unit 2). The details (open as much as you like) Strong beats want strong notes The working rule of nearly all bass playing: chord tones on the beats, anything you like between them . Beats 1 and 3 carry the harmony; land root/3rd/5th/7th there and the line sounds "inside" no matter what happens on the &s. This one rule generates everything from Motown to walking jazz. Thirds: the polite warning The third is gorgeous mid-bar and risky on the bottom of beat 1 (it makes the chord sound inverted — sometimes wanted, often not). Standard craft: root on the change, third later in the bar. When the chart WANTS the third under the chord it will say so with a slash (G/B). Exceptions are

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 5 · Bass Lines Song forms & charts Bands don't read novels on stage — they read maps. A chart is one page that holds a whole song, and knowing where you are on that map matters more than any individual note you play. Before you start Numerals & Nashville numbers , the repeat symbols (𝄆 𝄇, D.S., coda) — all tappable in the symbol guide. The common floor plans Form Shape Where you'll meet it 12-bar blues the map from last page blues, early rock, jazz jams Verse–chorus V C V C bridge C C most pop and rock since the 60s AABA ("32-bar") 8+8+8+8; B = "the bridge" jazz standards, early pop, showtunes Vamp / one-chord loop until cued funk, gospel, jam bands The bassist's form-skill is simple to state: always know which bar of which section you're in , and mark the seams — sections changing is when your register, density, or pattern should shift. Reading a real-world chart Here's the kind of thing that gets handed to you (or texted, 20 minutes before downbeat): "Midnight Sandwich" — med. funk ♩=96 key: Am INTRO | Am7 . . . | . . . . | (bass + drums only) VERSE 𝄆 Am7 . . . | D7 . . . 𝄇 ×4 CHORUS 𝄆 F . . . | G . . . | Am7 . . . | . . . . 𝄇 ×2 BRIDGE | F . . . | E7 . . . | (build!) → VERSE ×2, CHORUS ×2, then D.S. al Coda ⊕ CODA | Am7 . . . | (ring) Dots mean "keep playing the last chord, one per beat". Count bars in sections , not from the top: "verse, bar 3 of 8" survives distractions; "bar 51" doesn't. Pencil beats paper: circle the landmine bars (the quick change, the odd 2-bar extension). (in Am thinking) — or in relative-C thinking, 6− and 2. Numbers mean the singer can move the key at soundcheck and nobody rewrites anything. If you've interna

h: The classic line every bassist owns
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 5 · Bass Lines The 12-bar blues — everyone's common language Walk into any jam on the planet, say "blues in A", count four — and total strangers play a song together. This is the form that makes that possible. Learn it until it's not memory but geography. Before you start I, IV, V and dominant 7ths . Blues bends one rule: ALL three chords are played as dominant 7s. The map I7 I7 I7 I7 IV7 IV7 I7 I7 V7 IV7 I7 V7 (turnaround) Three four-bar sentences: make yourself at home (4 bars) — go visit the IV (2) and come back (2) — build the tension (V–IV) and resolve, with the last bar ( turnaround ) launching the next chorus. In A: A7 / D7 / E7. In G: G7 / C7 / D7. The Roman numerals are the form; keys are just wardrobe. Hear the form ▶ One chorus in A (roots) ■ Stop Follow the highlight and say the numerals out loud: "one… one… one… one… FOUR, four, one, one, FIVE, four, one, five!" The classic line every bassist owns The root–3–5–6 "boogie" pattern, one bar per chord, moved between I, IV, and V — the sound of ten thousand records: ▶ Play Because it's built from intervals, the identical shape works from A (fret 5), D (A-string 5), and E (A-string 7) — one pattern, entire form covered. The details (open as much as you like) The quick change (and other dialects) Common variants you'll meet: quick change — bar 2 goes to IV7 (…and bar 1 stays I7); slow blues in 12/8 — same map, rolling triplet feel; minor blues — i7, iv7, V7 (only the V stays dominant); 8-bar blues — a compressed cousin. Learn the standard form cold and the dialects are one-sentence updates. Why dominant 7ths everywhere doesn't break theory By diatonic rules only V gets a dominant 7 — blues ignores this and puts that restless ♭7 buzz on every chord. That's not sloppy harmony; it IS the blues sound: three chords that all lean, forever unresolved. Theory describes; blues predates the description. Turnaround vocabulary, starter kit Bar 12's job is re-launch. Three reliable moves: sit on V7; walk up to it (I–i

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 5 · Bass Lines Walking bass 101 A note on every beat, strolling through the changes like someone who knows the neighborhood. It looks improvised — it's actually a formula you can learn this week, then spend years making elegant. Before you start Chord tones and approach notes — walking is those two pages at a steady quarter-note pulse. The formula (really) For each bar of a chord, four quarter notes: The beginner's recipe Beat 1: the root — non-negotiable, for now. Beats 2–3: chord tones or scale steps heading somewhere (up or down, pick a direction per bar). Beat 4: an approach note aimed at the next bar's root. That's it. R–2–3–approach, R–3–5–approach, R–5–3–approach… every combination of "start home, travel, aim" produces a legitimate walking line. Swing the eighth-note feel later; make the quarters even first. A written chorus to steal ▶ Play the line Dm7–G7–C: watch beat 4 of every bar do the aiming. Steal this exact line, then vary one beat at a time. The three classic bar-shapes Shape Example over C Feel Scalar R-2-3-app C D E (F♯) smooth stroll upward Arpeggio R-3-5-app C E G (A♭ or B) spells the chord clearly Down R-♭7-5-app C B♭ G (F♯) descending gravity (great on 7th chords) The details (open as much as you like) Two chords per bar (the ii–V squeeze) Real charts often put two chords in one bar (Dm7 G7 | C). Simple rule: root on 1, root on 3 , with beats 2 and 4 as approaches. When in doubt, roots-only through fast changes is professional, not remedial — clarity beats cleverness at speed. Making it swing (after it's even) Jazz quarters aren't literally even — they lean slightly long-short with a fat, connected attack (that's the swing you met in the reading unit; the styles unit drills it). But the drill order is strict: metronome-even first, feel second. A lopsided beat can't be styled. Where walking actually gets used Jazz standards, obviously — but also blues shuffles, rockabilly, country two-beat turnarounds, gospel, and the last chorus of a thou

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 6 · Styles Blues shuffle — the long-short skip Take even eighths and lean them: LOOONG-short, LOOONG-short. That lean is swing , the shuffle is its strut, and the 12-bar form from Unit 5 is its home address. Before you start The 12-bar form and its boogie line — this page changes the FEEL of what you already play. The feel, explained honestly Under a shuffle, each beat divides into a triplet ("1-trip-let"): you play the first and third slices and skip the middle — long-short, roughly 2:1. Nobody counts that fraction while playing; you install it by feel. Say "HUM-pty DUM-pty" in rhythm — that's a shuffle. The demos here play it; your job is to lock to it until straight eighths feel like a choice, not a default. The groove ▶ Play (listen to the lean) Written as triplet-feel eighths — the notation shows pairs; the LEAN is applied by the player (and by this site's playback here). Play along: groove machine — shuffle blues in A . Shuffle craft The boogie line shuffled (R-3-5-6 in pairs) is the canonical part — bar for bar through the whole 12-bar form. Two-feel option: for verses, drop to roots on 1 and 3 (like a slow heartbeat), then kick into the full shuffle for solos — instant arrangement. The short note is quiet. The skip reads as feel when the "short" is lighter; equal volume turns the strut into a limp. Slow blues = 12/8 : the same triplet DNA at ballad tempo, all six slices felt, the mightiest groove in the idiom. The details (open as much as you like) How swung is swung? The 2:1 triplet split is the schoolbook answer; real players slide between nearly-straight (fast tempos flatten the swing) and harder than 2:1 (slow, greasy shuffles). The band agrees by ear in the first bar. Your job is matching the drummer's ride cymbal, not a fraction. h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 6 · Styles Funk — the sixteenth-note grid Funk slices every beat into four and then plays hide-and-seek on the grid: a few fat notes, a few muted ghosts , and a lot of exact silence. It's the most rhythmically precise style on this site — and the most fun. Before you start Counting "1 e & a" , note-length control , and fretting-hand muting . The three ingredients The 16th grid — felt continuously ("1 e & a 2 e & a"), even though you play maybe five of sixteen slots. Ghost notes (×) — fretting hand touches, doesn't press; plucking hand plays full strength. The "thunk" that fills the machine between real notes. The One. Funk's law: whatever happens mid-bar, beat 1 lands HUGE. James Brown built an empire on it. The groove ▶ Play The ×-headed notes are ghosts — rhythm without pitch. Play along: groove machine — funk vamp on E7 . Funk harmony (mercifully simple) Whole songs sit on one dominant-7th or minor-7th vamp — harmony parks so rhythm can drive. Your note pool: root, octave, ♭7, 5 — the minor pentatonic with the root doing most shifts. The complexity budget goes entirely to when , not what . The details (open as much as you like) Ghost-note mechanics, precisely Fretting fingers rest on the string (multiple fingers = deader = better), zero pressure toward the fret; plucking hand strikes normally. The pitch disappears, the rhythm stays. Drill: four plucks alternating real-ghost-real-ghost on one fret until the ghosts are effortless. Then they start sneaking into everything you play, which is correct. Where slap fits Slap (thumb-strike + finger-pop) is a funk sound , not a requirement — Rocco Prestia and Jamerson played monumental funk fingerstyle. The technique gets its own treatment in the Expert unit; the grid and ghosts you build here are 90% of what makes slap lines work anyway. Listening list James Brown "Sex Machine" (Bootsy's one-emphasis), Tower of Power "What Is Hip?" (Rocco's relentless 16ths), Sly & the Family Stone "Thank You" (the slap prototype), V
Sixteen sixteenths alternating full notes and ghosts on one fret: the fat notes identical, the ghosts pitchless thunks, the grid metronomic — at 90 bpm.

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 6 · Styles Jazz walking — the conversation Unit 5 taught the walking formula. Jazz is where the formula becomes speech: swung, connected, endlessly varied, in dialogue with the ride cymbal. This page upgrades the recipe into the style. Before you start Walking 101 cold, and the swing feel in your body. What changes from Walking 101 The feel: quarters swing — fat, connected ( legato ), each note leaning into the next. The classic instruction: "play the quarter notes long". The recipe loosens: beat 1 can now be a third or fifth if the line demands it (the ear you've built decides). Enclosures join the vocabulary: surround the target from both sides (above, below, land) — the chromatic approach's fancy sibling. Skips for drama: an octave leap or a big drop resets the line's register — walking isn't only stepwise. The drummer is your duet partner: your quarters and the ride cymbal's "ding ding-a-ding" are one instrument. The line ▶ Play (swung) Bars: Cmaj7 · Am7 · Dm7 · G7 — the "rhythm changes" turnaround. Spot the enclosure into bar 3. Play along: groove machine — walking ii–V–I in C . The details (open as much as you like) Long quarters: the sound of jazz bass Classical players separate; jazz walkers connect. Each quarter rings right up to the next attack — on upright it's the natural sound, on bass guitar you create it: fretting hand holds, plucking alternation smooth, no gaps. Record two bars staccato then legato: the second one is jazz. Skip-a-beat variations (the drop and the push) Once steady quarters are effortless, jazz decorates them: the occasional two eighth-notes on beat 4 (a little run into the next bar), a triplet drag, or a dead-note skip. Rule of taste: one decoration per four bars, and never at the expense of the quarter pulse. The pulse IS the gig. Listening list Ray Brown with Oscar Peterson ("Night Train" — the definition), Paul Chambers on "Kind of Blue" (walking as architecture), Ron Carter with Miles ("So What" live versions), Christian McB

h: The anatomy of the one drop
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 6 · Styles Reggae — the groove of what you don't play Reggae bass is huge, melodic, and mostly silence. The radical move: beat one is often empty — the whole feel hangs from the spaces. If funk taught precision, reggae teaches trust. Before you start Note-length control and rests as actions ( rhythm unit ). The anatomy of the one drop "One drop" = the drums drop beat 1 (kick + rim land on beat 3). The bass often rests on 1 too — the bar starts with air. The bass phrase is a melody : short, fat, repeated exactly, usually from root–fifth–octave and the minor pentatonic . Fat and short: palm-muted or fleshy-thumbed notes, deep tone (neck-position pluck, tone rolled off), endings placed as carefully as attacks. Repetition is sacred. The same two-bar phrase for five minutes IS the arrangement — variation is a special event. The groove ▶ Play Feel the empty ONE. Play along: groove machine — one drop on A (pads play the off-beat skank). The details (open as much as you like) Surviving the empty beat 1 Everything you've trained says "play the one" — reggae says feel it and stay silent. The trick: keep counting out loud, treat the silence as a note you're playing (it has an exact start!), and anchor to the guitar's off-beat "skank" on 2 and 4. When the empty One stops feeling like falling, you've got it. Variants: steppers & rockers Not all reggae drops the one: "steppers" puts the kick on every beat (four on the floor); "rockers" adds syncopated punch. The bass principles — melodic phrase, fat-short notes, sacred repetition — hold across all of them. Listening list Bob Marley "Natural Mystic" & "Stir It Up" (Aston "Family Man" Barrett — the vocabulary), Toots & the Maytals "Pressure Drop", Gregory Isaacs "Night Nurse" (Flabba Holt), Black Uhuru "Guess Who's Coming to Dinner" (Robbie Shakespeare). h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 6 · Styles Rock — the straight-eighth engine Driving, even eighth notes on the root, welded to the kick drum. It sounds like the simplest thing on this site — and playing it great for three minutes is a genuine skill. Before you start Even eighths with strict alternation and the accent control from timing & groove . The recipe Eighth-note roots , relentless and even — the engine room of AC/DC, Tom Petty, punk, and most of the radio. Note length is the sound : connected-but-separate, each note handed to the next. All-legato turns to mush; too staccato turns nervous. Dynamics sit at a strong medium , with accents (beat 1, or wherever the kick lands) not extra speed. Changes announced on beat 1 , sometimes approached with a quick scale walk in the last beat. The groove ▶ Play Then play along with the band: groove machine — rock 8ths, I–V–vi–IV in C . The details (open as much as you like) Pick vs fingers (the honest answer) Plenty of rock is played with a pick: brighter attack, natural evenness at speed, and the downstroke chug is its own sound. Fingers get you 95% of the way with more tonal range. If you're curious, learn downstrokes-only first (the punk engine), then alternate picking. It's an accent, not an identity. Variations that keep eighths interesting The craft is micro-variation: drop to the fifth for the bar's second half; lift to the octave for a chorus; a quick 3-note scale walk into the next chord (the approach-note page, faster); one strategic push (hitting the change an eighth early, with the kick). One variation per section — rock rewards restraint with size. Listening list (find these bass lines) AC/DC "Back in Black" (the discipline), Tom Petty "American Girl" (eighths with lift), Ramones — any track (downstroke commitment), Foo Fighters "Everlong" (eighths as architecture), Queen "Another One Bites the Dust" (when rock borrows funk). Listen for note LENGTH as much as pitch. Practice Check your understanding Where this goes next Bend those strai

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 6 · Styles Soul & Motown — the singable bass line In 1960s Detroit, James Jamerson turned the bass part into a second melody — syncopated, chromatic, joyful — without ever dropping the floor. This page is that vocabulary: lines you could hum. Before you start The approach-note grammar and the dotted-quarter + eighth rhythm — soul runs on both. The vocabulary The pulse: the dotted-quarter + eighth ("1 … &-of-2") and its variants — momentum without sixteenth-note anxiety. Chromatic connections everywhere: Jamerson walks between chord tones through every crack in the fretboard — approach notes as a lifestyle. Open strings as springboards — quick open-string eighths between fretted notes keep lines flowing (and famously let one hand do impossible things). Melody, but never at the floor's expense: roots still own the changes; the singing happens between them. The groove ▶ Play Play along: groove machine — Motown pulse, I–vi–IV–V in G . The details (open as much as you like) The Jamerson recipe, reverse-engineered Take the plain root-fifth skeleton. Now: replace straight repeats with the dotted pulse; connect every change chromatically; drop an open-string eighth before big jumps; add ONE ghosted sixteenth per phrase; end phrases on the third for sweetness. That's a caricature of genius — but it's a playable one, and the exercises drill each move. Muted thump: the era's tone Sixties records: flatwound strings, foam under the bridge, plucked with fingers or thumb — a warm, short thump. Get 80% of it today with the palm mute and the tone knob rolled halfway down. Note length does the rest. Listening list The Temptations "My Girl" (the walk-up as hook), Stevie Wonder "For Once in My Life" (Jamerson unleashed), Marvin Gaye "What's Going On" (the ballad masterpiece), Aretha Franklin "Respect" (Tommy Cogbill), Al Green "Love and Happiness" (Memphis restraint). Hum the bass lines — you'll find you already can. Practice Check your understanding Where this goes next Take away

h: How to get good at this
b: Unit 7 · Practice Labs Ear trainer The bridge between hearing music and playing it. Five minutes a day of interval and chord-quality recognition compounds faster than any other practice on this site. Intervals Triad quality Seventh chords ascending descending both at once ▶ Play a question ↻ Hear it again Press play to get a question. correct 0 / 0 streak 0 How to get good at this Start with the checked subset (thirds, fourth, fifth, octave) — add intervals only when you're above ~80%. Replays are free. Listen as many times as you want; guessing fast teaches guessing. Sing before you answer. Matching the notes with your voice, even badly, is what wires them in — the song-anchor table helps. Descending is its own skill. Most people learn ascending first and are surprised; train both.

Unit 7 · Practice Labs Fretboard trainer Sixty seconds, one neck, no mercy. Name marked notes or find prompted ones — misses become flashcards automatically, so the trainer and your review deck conspire against your weak spots.
