Name the note Find the note Frets 0–5 Frets 0–12 60-second round ▶ Start round Name the marked note. Press start. score 0 streak 0 best — time 60 How to use it Start on frets 0–5, "name" mode. That region holds most of early bass life; get it under 2 seconds per note. "Find" mode is the inverse skill — it's what you use when reading charts. Both directions matter. Think landmarks, not counting : the dots, the 5th-fret rule, octave shapes. If you're counting from the nut, slow down and re-anchor. Wrong answers feed your flashcards — the notes that fool you will keep visiting until they don't.

Unit 7 · Practice Labs Groove machine A patient band that never gets bored: pick a progression, a style, and a tempo — then play along. The chart lights up, the fretboard shows the current chord's notes, and the whole thing loops until you stop. progression key style pattern tempo 90 custom chords (bars split by |) ▶ Play count-in chord pads bass cues choose your band… Amber = the current chord's root, everywhere it lives; outlined = its other chord tones. Tap any fret to hear it. How to practice with it Match the cues first — play exactly what the bass cues play until the pattern is in your hands, then turn "bass cues" off and own it alone. Roots-only is a complete workout : any progression, roots on beat 1, until the changes stop surprising you. Steal the styles : each Unit 6 page deep-links here with its groove pre-configured. Gap training : turn pads AND cues off — just clicks and the highlighted chart. You are the harmony now. Presets to try Blues shuffle in A · Pop-rock I–V–vi–IV in C · Walking ii–V–I in F · Funk vamp on E7 · One-drop doo-wop in G

Unit 7 · Practice Labs Routine builder Tell it how long you've got; it assembles a session from the exercises you've been neglecting — never-practiced first, then the stalest — bookended by a warmup and something fun. Print it and put it on the music stand. 10 min 20 min 30 min 45 min Build my session 🖨 Print it it reads your practice log and page progress — nothing leaves your browser How it chooses Exercises from pages you've completed or visited come first — the builder follows your path, it doesn't set one. Within those: never-practiced beats stale beats recent (it reads the "log practice" buttons you've been pressing). Every session starts with tuning + a hands warmup and ends with ears or free play — the session shape from the technique unit.

Unit 7 · Practice Labs Tuner Plug nothing in: this tuner listens through your device's microphone and shows exactly how far off each string is. (No mic? The reference pads below work by ear, like the tuning page taught.) 🎤 Enable microphone — -50¢ flat 0 +50¢ sharp the meter needs your permission to listen — or tune by ear with the pads below E 41.2 Hz A 55.0 Hz D 73.4 Hz G 98.0 Hz Getting a steady reading One string at a time , plucked over the end of the fingerboard (a rounder tone reads better), other strings muted. Let it ring — the reading settles about half a second after the attack. Tune UP into the note (drop below, then come up) so the string holds its pitch. Within ±5 cents the meter turns green — that's in tune for any practical purpose. Why your phone can do this The tuner measures how often the waveform repeats (your low E repeats 41.2 times per second) by comparing the signal against time-shifted copies of itself — the shift where they line up best IS the period. The same math from the fret-math page then turns frequency into the nearest note and the difference into cents (hundredths of a semitone).

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 8 · Expert Track Advanced techniques — slap, tap, harmonics, and the fifth string The flashy wing of the vocabulary. Everything here is optional; everything here is fun; none of it substitutes for the pocket you built in Units 4–6 — it decorates it. Before you start Solid 16th-grid + ghost notes — slap is 80% that skill wearing a leather jacket. Slap & pop, the honest primer The thumb (slap): strike the string with the side of the thumb's knuckle at the neck's end, bouncing OFF like a drumstick — the fret-clack is the sound. Rotate from the forearm, loose wrist. The finger (pop): index slips under the D or G string and yanks it to snap against the frets. Thumb-pop pairs are funk's snare-and-kick. Ghosts still rule: slap lines are mostly muted thumb/pop on the grid; the pitched notes are the minority. If your funk ghosts are solid, slap is a tone change, not a new skill. Start at 60 bpm , thumb-only on one muted string, even as a metronome. Speed is compound interest on evenness. Tapping, harmonics, palm+thumb Tapping: fretting-hand hammer-ons taken to their conclusion — both hands hammer notes on the fingerboard. Party trick until it's musical; the Unit 4 hammer-on drill is its foundation. Natural harmonics: touch (don't press) directly over fret 12, 7, or 5 and pluck — bell tones. The 5th/7th-fret bells are also a tuning cross-check and the intro to "Portrait of Tracy". Palm mute + thumb: not advanced at all, but paired with these it completes the tonal palette — thump, bell, clack, snap. The fifth string (and beyond) A 5-string adds a low B — five more semitones of floor (B0 ≈ 30.9 Hz) and, just as valuable, positional reach : the notes under your hand span more range without shifting. Everything on this site transfers: the B string is a fourth below E, so every shape, interval, and rule works unchanged (the theory library on this site already knows the tuning). Real adjustments: right-hand muting gets stricter (a floating thumb helps — the right-hand page fl
Two minutes of muted thumb-slaps on the E string at 60 bpm — even as a drum machine, bouncing off the string, wrist loose enough to shake water off.
Thumb on the root (A, E-string 5… any root), pop on its octave — the classic pair, clean and even at 76 bpm, then in the disco-octave pattern from Unit 5.
Clear bells at frets 12, 7, and 5 on every string — touch point exact, release immediate — plus the E7/A5 tuning cross-check performed once.

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 8 · Expert Track Chord extensions & alterations C13♯11 looks like a password. It's actually a polite stack of thirds with two notes tweaked — and the bassist's response to almost all of it is beautifully simple. Before you start Seventh chords and their symbols, cold. The idea Keep stacking thirds past the 7th and you get the extensions : the 9th (a 2nd an octave up), 11th (a 4th up high), 13th (a 6th up high). They add color, not function — a C13 still behaves like a C7 that dressed up. Alterations bend extensions or the 5th by a semitone (♭9, ♯9, ♯11, ♭13, ♭5, ♯5) — mostly on dominant chords, to sharpen their pull. Reading the passwords Symbol Means Bassist's note C9 C7 + 9 root (the 9 is upstairs decor) Cmaj9 Cmaj7 + 9 root C13 C7 + 9 + 13 root; ♭7 and 3 in fills C7♭9 / C7♯9 dominant with bent 9 root; expect a dark resolve C7alt dominant, everything bent (♭9 ♯9 ♯5…) root, then get out of the way Cadd9 triad + 9, no 7th root; pop sparkle, not jazz tension C6/9 triad + 6 + 9 root; the "smiling ending" chord The liberating rule Numbers above 7 are the pianist's and guitarist's business. Your root (and 3rd/♭7 when you choose) supports every one of them. Never fight the upstairs — a low 9th or 11th under the band turns color into mud. Big chords ask the bass for MORE simplicity, not less. Hear the stack grow C7 C9 C13 C7♭9 Same bass note under all four — listen to the upstairs change while your job doesn't. The details (open as much as you like) Why the 11th is the troublemaker Over a major 3rd, the natural 11 sits a semitone above it — the one rub that reads as error rather than color. Hence ♯11 on major chords (the Lydia­n note) and plain 11 only on minor chords (where the ♭3 leaves room). If you ever wondered why "sus" chords remove the 3rd: it's to make room for exactly this note. When the bass DOES play extensions High-register fills, chordal moments (solo bass, duos), and melodic hooks — deliberately, upstairs, where they belong. The 9th two octaves above t
Ten scary symbols (Dm9, G13, Cmaj9, A7alt, F6/9, E7♯9, Bm11, Ab13, D7♭9, Gadd9) each decoded in seconds: root, family (maj/min/dom), and your first-choice note.

t: Practice: One upstairs fill, placed right
b: Over a Cmaj9 vamp: root groove for three bars, then one fill touching the 9 (D) UP an octave-plus — color without mud, landed back on the root.

h: The method (four passes over any chart)
The common "visitors" (non-diatonic chords with day jobs)
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 8 · Expert Track Harmonic analysis — reading the story Given any chord chart, you can now name the pieces. Analysis is naming the plot : which chords are home, which are travel, which are visitors from other keys — so your line can tell the same story. Before you start Diatonic families , the circle , and Roman-numeral fluency. The method (four passes over any chart) Find the key — key signature, first/last chords, and where things feel resolved. Write the numerals over the letters. Mark the functions : home (I, vi), departure (ii, IV), tension (V, vii°). Every progression is trips between these three rooms. Flag the visitors — chords that don't fit the key (see below). They're the interesting bits. Find the bass story : strongest root motions (5ths falling), stepwise walks, pedal points. Your line should agree with the plot you just read. The common "visitors" (non-diatonic chords with day jobs) Visitor Example in C What it's doing Secondary dominant D7 → G (written V7/V) borrowing V's pull to aim at a non-tonic chord Borrowed iv Fm in C major the bittersweet minor-plagal move (Beatles everywhere) ♭VII B♭ in C major the rock backdoor (borrowed from C minor) Diminished passer C♯° between C and Dm chromatic stepping-stone between diatonic chords Tritone sub D♭7 instead of G7 jazz swap — same tritone engine, chromatic bass fall Worked example | C | E7 | Am | C7 | I V7/vi vi V7/IV ← the E7 and C7 are visitors: | F | Fm | C G | C | each one a dominant aimed IV iv I V I at the NEXT chord borrowed! ↑ ▶ Hear it (roots + pads) Listen for how E7 makes Am feel inevitable, and how Fm aches on the way home. That's function, audible. The details (open as much as you like) What analysis changes about your playing Tension chords get leading approaches (that's what they're FOR); home chords get planted roots; secondary dominants beg for chromatic walks into their targets; a tritone sub means your bass gets a chromatic descent for free (D♭ falling to C). Analysis isn't homework

t: Practice: Play the worked example with intent
b: The 8-bar example played with function-aware choices: planted roots on I, chromatic approaches into each visitor's target, the Fm bar milked for its ache.

t: Practice: Analyze three songs you already play
b: Three real charts marked up with numerals + functions + circled visitors, and one sentence each about what the bass should therefore do.

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 8 · Expert Track Improvisation & fills Improvising isn't inventing from nothing — it's recombining vocabulary you own, inside a form you trust, with taste as the filter. You already have all three pieces; this page teaches the recombining. Before you start Chord-tone fluency ( Unit 5 ), the pentatonic boxes , and a few stolen lines in the notebook. Fills: the gateway improvisation A fill is a one-or-two-beat decoration at a phrase boundary. The rules of the craft: Location: ends of 4- and 8-bar phrases, section seams, under held vocal notes — the built-in parking spots. Aim: a fill is an approach note with ambition — it must LAND (usually on the next downbeat's root). Material: pentatonic fragments, chord-tone runs, chromatic walks, one octave pop — the vocabulary you have. Budget: one fill per phrase maximum. The bar you DON'T fill is what makes the one you do sound intentional. The fill workshop ▶ Play all three Three shapes into the same Am landing: scale walk, pentatonic tumble, chromatic slide. Steal, then vary. Building a solo (yes, you) Bass solos are rarer than guitar solos, and better for it. The method that works the first time: sing a phrase, play what you sang, breathe, repeat. The voice can't play anything your taste doesn't approve, so the filter is built in. Structure it like speech: statement, variation, contrast, home — over a form you know cold (the 12-bar is perfect). The details (open as much as you like) Motif thinking: one idea, four ways Great improvisers develop, not enumerate. Take three notes (say R–♭3–4). Play them. Then: same rhythm higher; same notes different rhythm; backwards; leave a hole where a note was. One cell, one chorus of music — and listeners hear intent instead of scales. Question & answer with yourself Two-bar question (ends up in the air — off the root), two-bar answer (comes home). Loop the groove machine's vamp and hold this conversation for ten minutes. It's the fastest route to solos with shape — and secretly it's
Eight bars looping (groove machine, any preset): solid line for 3½ bars, ONE fill into bar 5's downbeat, repeat — ten minutes, every fill landing.
One 12-bar chorus built phrase by phrase: sing two bars, play those two bars, until a complete sung-then-played solo exists — taste guaranteed by your own voice.

h: The two ways to think about modes
Hear a mode change the same root
The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 8 · Expert Track Modes — seven flavors of one scale You met the idea in Unit 3: same notes, different home. Now make it practical — because two of the seven modes (Dorian and Mixolydian) quietly run most of the funk, rock, and jam-band music you play. Before you start Major scale and relative thinking . From here on, standard terminology leads — every term still taps. The two ways to think about modes Derivative (good for finding notes): D Dorian = the C major scale played from D. Parallel (good for hearing): D Dorian = D natural minor with the 6th raised. The first gets your fingers there; the second tells your ears what changed. Experts flip between both without noticing. The working bassist's mode table Mode Formula vs major Flavor · where it lives Ionian (major) — home base; pop, everything Dorian ♭3 ♭7 minor-but-hopeful; funk vamps, "So What", Santana Phrygian ♭2 ♭3 ♭6 ♭7 Spanish/metal darkness Lydian ♯4 floating film-score major Mixolydian ♭7 major with a blues collar; rock & dominant-7 vamps Aeolian (nat. minor) ♭3 ♭6 ♭7 the default minor; you know it Locrian ♭2 ♭3 ♭5 ♭6 ♭7 home of m7♭5; rarely a resting place Bold rows are the daily drivers. When a one-chord vamp is minor 7, think Dorian; when it's dominant 7, think Mixolydian — those raised 6ths and lowered 7ths are what keep long vamps from going stale. Hear a mode change the same root Ionian Dorian Mixolydian Aeolian Phrygian All from root A — the drone stays, the flavor moves. Press a mode to hear it over the root. The details (open as much as you like) When modes actually matter to a bassist Honestly: on vamps and modal tunes — one chord for a long time, where the color notes you choose (the Dorian 6th, the Mixolydian ♭7) ARE the harmony. On fast-changing progressions, chord tones + approach grammar matter far more than mode names. Modes are seasoning for harmonic standstill. Hearing them fast: the one-note trick Each daily-driver mode differs from plain major/minor by ONE note: Mixolydian = major

t: Practice: Four flavors over one drone
b: Over an A drone (tuning-page style or the demo): Ionian, Mixolydian, Dorian, Aeolian played in sequence, the changed notes named out loud as they pass.
Two minutes over an Am7 vamp using the Dorian 6th (F♯) as a deliberate color — placed, not stumbled on — at 94 bpm.

h: The one non-negotiable: play with people
Where to go deeper (a short, opinionated list)
b: Unit 8 · Expert Track Next steps — the map for the years after This site can take you from "which one is the E string?" to walking a jazz blues. What it can't do is be a band. Here's the honest map of what comes next, and how everything you built here keeps compounding. The one non-negotiable: play with people Every skill on this site exists for the moment another human counts to four. Nothing — not one lab, not one exercise — substitutes for it, and nothing accelerates you faster. Bassists have a structural advantage: everyone needs one . Realistic entry points: Jams and open mics — blues jams especially: the form is the 12-bar you own, and "blues in A?" is a complete rehearsal. One other person — a guitarist or pianist friend and a chart. Duos expose everything kindly. Community ensembles, church bands, school groups — structured, forgiving, regular. Regular beats impressive. Online collaboration — trading stems counts; timing against someone else's take is real ensemble skill. Your weekly diet (the maintenance version) Daily-ish: the routine builder keeps technique and repertoire honest in 20 minutes. Weekly: one steal ; a few minutes of ear trainer ; one groove-machine session in a style you're worst at. Monthly: record yourself playing a full song and listen like a producer. The recording is still the honest mirror. Where to go deeper (a short, opinionated list) Reading: Standing in the Shadows of Motown (Jamerson transcriptions — a bible), Ed Friedland's Building Walking Bass Lines , Rufus Reid's The Evolving Bassist (upright perspective that improves electric players). Listening as study: pick one bassist per month and binge them: Jamerson → Jaco → Duck Dunn → Carol Kaye → Paul McCartney → Bootsy → Pino Palladino → Esperanza Spalding. Steal one thing from each. A teacher, occasionally: even two or three lessons a year from a good teacher catches habits no website can see. Bring them a recording of yourself; that's where lessons start mattering. What "expert"

h: The details (open as much as you like)
b: Unit 8 · Expert Track Transcription — stealing like a musician Lifting lines off records by ear is the single highest-value practice habit in existence: it trains ears, hands, vocabulary, and feel in one activity. Here's the method that makes it merely hard instead of impossible. Before you start Some ear-trainer mileage — intervals at ~80% — and the fretboard map . The method Choose winnable battles : clear mixes, moderate tempos, lines you already half-hum. (Motown > metal for audibility.) Loop tiny chunks — two bars at most. Any looping player works; slow-downers (browser extensions, most streaming apps' 0.75×) are legal performance-enhancers. Sing it first. If you can't sing the chunk, you haven't heard it yet — keep looping BEFORE touching the bass. Find the first note (hunt on the neck), then use interval ears for the rest: "up a fourth… down a step…". Get the rhythm honest : count it out loud against the record; ghost notes and rests are part of the line. Write it down — tab, notation, or your own scrawl. Writing is what moves it from "solved" to "owned". Play along until invisible : your line and the record become one sound. That's the rep that counts. The details (open as much as you like) A graded starter list Level 1 (roots, clear): Bill Withers "Lean On Me", Neil Young "Harvest Moon". Level 2 (moving lines): "Stand By Me", "My Girl", "With or Without You". Level 3 (vocabulary): "What's Going On", "Come Together", "Money". Level 4 (grids & ghosts): "Sir Duke", "What Is Hip?", anything Jamerson that scares you. One song at your edge beats five beneath it. When you're stuck on a note Check the octave (bass fundamentals fool ears — try the same fret 12 higher); check for open strings (that resonance is a fingerprint); ask the harmony (it's probably a chord tone — which chord is passing?); and suspect ghost notes when the rhythm has more attacks than pitches. Still stuck? Move on and return — ears grow overnight, literally. Tab sites vs. your ears Checking a
One Level-1/2 song lifted completely by ear, written down in any notation, and played along with the record until the two blur.
Four consecutive weeks, one transcribed line (or riff, or fill) per week — logged here — until stealing is just something you do.

t: Session 1 · Day one: make it rumble
b: Session 1 · Phase 1 — First sounds Day one: make it rumble Bass in hand? Good. In the next twenty minutes you'll make your first sounds, tune them by ear, and play a real four-string bass line with a steady pulse. Reading about music starts later — today you play. Get ready One minute, tops — then you're playing. Sit or stand — your call. If you have a strap, set it so the bass hangs at the same height either way; future-you will be glad the two feel identical. Body against you, neck angled slightly up. Sitting: the bass's waist rests on your right thigh. Right forearm rests on the top edge of the body, hand hanging relaxed over the strings. Left hand cradles the neck — thumb on the back, fingers curled loosely over the strings. It does nothing else today. No amp? No problem. Unplugged is plenty loud for everything in this session. Playing left-handed? Mirror everything, today and always. Go deeper: the parts of the bass, and holding it well → Make your first sound Rest your right thumb on the pickup — the plastic-looking bar sitting under the strings. Now put the side of your index fingertip against the thickest string , and pull across it, toward the floor-side string — not up and away. Let it ring. That low rumble is the open E — the lowest note your bass has, and the foundation half the songs you know are standing on. Tune up You've made four sounds — now make them the right four sounds. Tap a pad, play the matching string, and turn that string's peg a little at a time until the two sounds melt into one. Tighter = higher, looser = lower. Can't tell higher from lower yet? Tune your string clearly too low, then come up slowly until the wobble dies. Every bassist started there. Go deeper: tuning, beats, and the 5th-fret self-check → · or use the microphone tuner Your first bass line Four strings, one pattern, still no left hand. Count "1 2 3 4" out loud — one pluck per count, and change strings each time you say "1". Why that worked You just played the four open st

t: Practice: Four strings, one at a time
b: Each open string rings long and clean — no buzz, no double-hit — and you name it as it sounds.
Sixteen even plucks — a bar each on E, A, D, then A — with every string change landing exactly on the count of 1.

t: Session 2 · Two fingers, one steady beat
b: Session 2 · Phase 1 — First sounds Two fingers, one steady beat Yesterday you plucked however your hand felt like it. Today your plucking hand gets its engine — two fingers taking turns — and the metronome stops being a button and becomes a bandmate. Tune up First thing, every time — thirty seconds now buys honest feedback all session. Warm-up: the four-string tour Session 1's riff, once through, counting out loud. Care about the change landing on "1" more than about speed. ▶ Hear it New thing: the two-finger engine Anchor your thumb on the pickup. Now pluck the open E with your index finger, then your middle finger, then index, then middle — like walking, finger after finger. Each fingertip pulls across the string and comes to rest against your thumb-side… and that's the whole technique. Tap ♩ 70 in the bar below and make every pluck land exactly on a click. Why that worked Two fingers taking strict turns is called alternation , and it's the engine behind almost everything a fingerstyle bassist plays: one finger doing all the work hits a speed ceiling in days; two never do. Plucking across the string (a rest stroke , landing on the next string) is what makes the note thick instead of twangy. And the number on the metronome? Tempo , in beats per minute — 70 means seventy clicks each minute, and your job is to sit so exactly on them that the click seems to disappear into your note. Players call that swallowing the click . Which finger starts? And where should the thumb go later? Nobody checks which finger starts — pick whichever, then alternate strictly even when a pattern would be easier repeating one finger; that's what builds the reflex. The thumb anchored on the pickup is the day-one home; later it learns to float onto the E string to quiet it while you play higher strings. That trick — and where exactly to pluck for fat vs. punchy — lives in the right-hand page below. Go deeper: the right hand → · timing & groove → Apply it: cross the strings Same engine, now to

t: Practice: The two-finger engine, on one string
b: A full minute of open-E quarter notes on the click — index-middle-index-middle without breaking pattern, and no pluck louder than its neighbors.
The tour of all four strings with alternation surviving every crossing — whichever finger is due next takes the new string, no double-plucks at the seams.

h: New thing: press your first fret
Apply it: the bass checks its own tuning
b: Session 3 · Phase 1 — First sounds First frets, first landmarks Your left hand has been politely holding the neck for two sessions. Today it presses down — three notes on one string, a riff that actually sounds like a riff, and a trick that lets the bass tune itself. Tune up Warm-up: the engine, crossing strings One pass of Session 2's crossing drill — say "i, m" under your breath and don't let the alternation reset at the seams. ▶ Hear it New thing: press your first fret Look at the metal strips crossing the neck. Put a left-hand fingertip on the thickest string just behind the third strip — close behind it, not on top of it — and press with the finger's tip, arm hanging relaxed. Pluck. That clear note is a G . Slide up to just behind the fifth strip: that's an A . Any finger that reaches comfortably is the right finger today — fingering systems come later. Why that worked Each metal strip is a fret , and each fret raises the string by exactly one semitone — the smallest step in Western music. Open E, count three frets up: E → F → F♯ → G . Two more: A . The whole neck is just that one rule, repeated. The dots on frets 3, 5, 7 (then 9 and the double-dot 12) aren't decoration — they're the landmarks every bassist navigates by, and you just played two of them. Tap anywhere — every fret plays its note. Find G and A on the E string, then peek at fret 5 against the open A above it. Why the frets get closer together up the neck Each semitone multiplies the string's vibrating speed by the same amount (about 1.06×), so each fret has to shave off the same fraction of string — and a fraction of an ever-shorter string is an ever-smaller slice. Equal steps of pitch, shrinking steps of wood. The full math lives in strings & fret math . Go deeper: the left hand → Apply it: the bass checks its own tuning Here's the landmark trick: fret 5 of any string sounds the same note as the next open string . E at fret 5 is an A — the very note the open A string plays. If the pair wobbles, on
The riff loops with every fretted note ringing clear — no buzz, no thud — and your left hand staying relaxed enough to wave hello between bars.
Each fret-5 note and its neighboring open string ring as one sound — and when a pair wobbles, you know which peg to reach for.

h: New thing: hold the floor under two chords
b: Session 4 · Phase 1 — First sounds Your first bass line Riffs are fun; a bass line has a job. Today you hold down two chords the way a working bassist does, learn the job description, and discover you already played a three-chord song in Session 1 without knowing it. Tune up Bonus points: check E against A with the fret-5 trick from last time before you touch the pads. Warm-up: the box riff Session 3's E–G–A riff, twice through, left hand staying soft. ▶ Hear it New thing: hold the floor under two chords Imagine a guitarist swaying between an E chord and an A chord, four beats each. Your job: play the chord's name-note — E under the E chord, A under the A chord — and land the change exactly with them. One long note per chord is a complete, professional bass line. Really. Tap ▶ on the card to hear the chords ring over your two notes. Why that worked The note a chord is named after is its root , and playing roots on the beat is the bassist's actual job description: you're the floor the chords stand on and the pulse the band walks to — harmony's anchor and rhythm's anchor, in one instrument . That's why one whole note, placed exactly, beats a flurry of clever ones. Notice what mattered: the moment of the change . Nail the root on beat 1 of each new chord and everything sounds intentional; smear it and even right notes sound wrong. Will it always be the root? (a peek ahead) No — thirds, fifths and walking lines are coming. But the pros' secret is that even the fanciest line usually touches the root exactly where the ear needs the floor: beat 1, chord change. Learn the plain version so deeply that the fancy versions stay honest. The full argument lives in the bassist's job . Go deeper: the role of the bass → · roots & chord tones → Apply it: the three-chord song Now add D. E for a bar, A for a bar, D for a bar, back to A — in quarter notes it's exactly the four-string tour from Session 1 . You've been playing a song all along; now you know why it works. Push it: whole no
Eight bars of E and A in whole notes — every change landing dead on beat 1, every note held its full four counts, no gap and no early jump.
E–A–D–A in steady quarters with chord changes announced cleanly on every beat 1 — the four-string tour, now played as harmony on purpose.

Session 5 · Phase 1 — First sounds Count it out loud You've been feeling wholes, quarters and doubled-up notes for four sessions. Today they get their names, and you get the counting language — spoken out loud, yes, actually out loud — that lets you play a rhythm you've never heard. Tune up Warm-up: the three-chord song Session 4's E–A–D–A, one pass in quarters. Say the chord names on the changes. ▶ Hear it New thing: the value ladder One note — open A — four rhythms. Each bar holds the same four beats; the notes just slice them differently. Count out loud exactly as written under the notes: numbers on the beats, "&" (say "and") between them. Why that worked Every bar in 4/4 holds four beats , and note values just say how many of them a note keeps: a whole note keeps all four, a half keeps two, a quarter keeps one, and an eighth splits the beat in two — the doubled-up feel you found in Session 2, now with a name. Counting aloud works because your mouth is a better metronome than your mind: thinking drifts, speech doesn't. Every serious player counted aloud for years — the ones who skip it are the ones who rush. Dots and ties, in one breath A dot after a note stretches it by half again — a dotted half holds three beats. A tie (a curved line joining two notes) glues their lengths into one sound, which is how written music holds a note across a barline. You'll read both constantly; the full tour with played examples is in rhythm & counting . Go deeper: rhythm & counting → Apply it: holes and holds Real bass lines are made of notes and silences. A rest is a silence with a length — you count it, you just don't play it. Mute the string with a flat left hand the moment the rest starts. Bar 3 rests on beat 1 — the hardest beat to not play. Count louder there. (Reggae builds a whole style on that hole; you'll meet it in Session 28.) Quick check Wrap up Phase 1 complete. You tune by ear, your right hand has an engine, your left hand has landmarks, you know the job, and you sp
All four bars flow without a stumble while you count "1 & 2 & 3 & 4 &" out loud the whole way — the eighth-note bar strictly alternated.
Rests land as clean, counted silences — string muted dead, no ring-over — and the dotted half truly holds through beat 3.

t: Session 6 · The fretboard is a machine
b: Session 6 · Phase 2 — Fretboard & rhythm The fretboard is a machine Ninety-six places to put a finger looks like chaos. It's actually a machine with three rules — and once your hands know them, "where is C?" stops being a memory question and becomes a one-second calculation. Tune up Warm-up: the box riff Session 3's E–G–A riff, one relaxed pass — those two dots at frets 3 and 5 are about to grow into a whole navigation system. ▶ Hear it New thing: the octave jump Play open E. Now go two strings up (to the D string) and two frets up (fret 2) — play that. Same note, higher floor: an octave. That two-up-two-over jump keeps its shape everywhere on the neck . Try it from G (fret 3) and A (fret 5). Why that worked The machine's three rules: Rule 1 — each string is tuned five frets (a fourth) above its neighbor, which is why fret 5 always equals the next open string. Rule 2 — twelve frets up, the alphabet wraps: fret 12 is the same letter as the open string, one octave higher (that's the double dot). Rule 3 — because rules 1 and 2 never change, every shape you learn works from any starting note. The octave jump is just rule 1 twice (10 frets of pitch) plus 2 frets. Tap around the map — every E, A and D under fret 12 is marked: Notice the landmarks: dots at 3–5–7–9, double at 12. Low-string names at those dots (G–A–B on E; C–D–E on A) unlock almost every root you'll need for months. One sound, two names? The note between F and G answers to F♯ ("F sharp — F raised a fret") and G♭ ("G flat — G lowered a fret"). Same fret, same sound, two spellings; which name is "right" depends on the key you're in — that story starts in Session 12. Until then: sharps going up, flats coming down is a fine habit. Full tour in notes on the fretboard . Go deeper: strings & fret math → · notes on the fretboard → Apply it: the note hunt Use the rules, not memory: fret 5 = next open string, twelve = wrap, octave shape = two-and-two. Say each note's name as you land on it. Then make it a sport: the

t: Practice: Octave jumps from three landmarks
b: E, G and A each jump cleanly to their octave — two strings up, two frets over — with both notes ringing evenly and the low note damped once you land.

t: Practice: Find every A, then every D
b: Every A below fret 12 in one unbroken pass, names said aloud — then every D — using the rules (fret 5, fret 12, octave shape) instead of staring.

t: Session 7 · Read it: tab and staff
h: New thing: play from the numbers
Apply it: now read the dots
b: Session 7 · Phase 2 — Fretboard & rhythm Read it: tab and staff Bass has two written languages: tab's honest little numbers and the staff's floating dots. Today you play the same riff from both — and learn which language tells you what. Tune up Warm-up: octave jumps Two rounds of Session 6's E and G jumps — shape as one hand-frame, names out loud. ▶ Hear it New thing: play from the numbers Look at the four-line grid under the notation — that's tab . Four lines = your four strings, thickest E on the bottom . A number is a fret on that string; 0 means open. Read only the numbers for now and let your ear supply the rhythm from the ▶ button. Why that worked The two systems split the truth between them. Tab tells your hands where — string and fret, no ambiguity, which matters on bass because the same note lives in several places. The staff tells your ears what — pitch and rhythm. Bass reads the bass clef (the fancy "9" whose two dots hug the F line ): lines G–B–D–F–A bottom to top, spaces A–C–E–G. Your open strings on the staff: E hangs below on a ledger line, A sits under the bottom line, D takes the middle line, G the fourth line. Learn those four addresses and you can triangulate everything else. The internet-tab warning Most tab sites skip rhythm entirely — numbers with no lengths. Fine when you already know how the song goes; useless when you don't. That's why real charts pair tab with notation (like this site does), and why the staff is worth befriending even if tab stays your first language. Full comparison in tab ↔ notation . Go deeper: the staff & bass clef → · tab ↔ notation → Apply it: now read the dots A new phrase. This time work top-down: read the staff first — name each note from its line or space, find it on the neck yourself — and only then check your answer against the tab below it. Cover the tab line with a finger on the first pass. Honesty now = fluency later. Quick check Wrap up You can now be handed music and play it — numbers for the hands, dots fo
The riff played straight off the numbers — string changes and frets found from the tab alone, at a tempo where you never stop moving.

t: Practice: The answer, from the staff
b: Each note named from its line or space before your finger moves — tab used only to check, never to lead.

h: New thing: the three-chord song, waltzing
b: Session 8 · Phase 2 — Fretboard & rhythm Meters you'll actually meet Every bar so far held four beats. Today you play the same song with three, then with 6/8's two big lopes — and feel why the number at the front of the staff changes how your whole body counts. Tune up Warm-up: the climb Session 7's riff — read it off the tab one more time, counting "1 2 3 4". ▶ Hear it New thing: the three-chord song, waltzing Same E–A–D–A song as Session 4 — but now each bar holds three beats. Count " 1 2 3" with a fat accent on the 1, and feel how the music starts to sway instead of march. Why that worked The fraction at the start of the staff is the time signature : the top number says how many beats fill a bar , the bottom says which note value counts as one beat . 4/4 — four quarters — is so standard it's literally called common time. 3/4 is the waltz: three quarters, heavy downbeat , sway. What changed for you as a bassist? Only the count. The job — root on the 1, pulse locked — survives every meter . 6/8 vs 3/4 — the classic trap Both contain six eighth notes, so on paper they look like twins. The difference is grouping : 3/4 is three steady quarter beats (ONE-two-three), 6/8 is two big lopes of three eighths each (ONE-and-a-TWO-and-a). Count a 6/8 song in 3/4 and you'll fight the drummer all night. The details — plus what the bassist does in each — are in time signatures . Go deeper: time signatures → Apply it: the 6/8 lope Now feel the twin that isn't: six eighths grouped as two pulses. Count " 1 -2-3 4 -5-6", leaning on 1 and 4 — your hand rolls E–G–A up and down like a slow tide. Same three notes as the box riff — only the meter is new. That's deliberate: change one thing at a time. Quick check Wrap up March, waltz, lope — three body-feels, one job. When a chart says 3/4 or 6/8 you now know what your count does before a note is played. Next time: the five little shapes that turn the fretboard into a map of sounds — intervals, the atoms of everything melodic. Mark Session
E–A–D–A in 3/4 with an audible lean on every "1" — three even beats, no phantom fourth sneaking in at the barline.
Six eighths rolling as TWO pulses — the lean lands on 1 and 4, and a listener would sway, not march.

t: Session 9 · Interval shapes and their sounds
h: Apply it: hum first, then play
b: Session 9 · Phase 2 — Fretboard & rhythm Interval shapes and their sounds Five small hand-shapes, one bonus reach — that's the entire melodic alphabet of the bass. Learn each shape's sound today and you'll never again wonder what a line will sound like before you play it. Tune up Warm-up: the 6/8 lope One pass of last session's lope — lean on 1 and 4, let it sway. ▶ Hear it New thing: the shape tour Home base: A at fret 5 of the E string . Every pair in this tour starts there and reaches for one shape. Play each pair slowly, twice, and listen for its personality — solid, bright, hollow, sweet, sad, bluesy. Why that worked The distance between two notes is an interval , measured in semitones — and on bass, every interval is a shape that never changes. Next string same fret: a fourth (5 semitones, open and sturdy). Next string two frets up: a fifth (7 — the power-chord sound). Two strings and two frets: the octave (12 — "same but higher"). Next string one fret back : a major third (4, sweet); two back: a minor third (3, sad). Bonus reach — two strings straight up: a minor seventh (10, the funky one). Because the neck's rules never change, the shape is the sound. Grab any root, make the fifth shape, and you already know what you'll hear. All six targets from the A root. Tap them; say the interval, then the note name. Why "major", "minor", "perfect"? Thirds, sixths and sevenths come in two sizes — the bigger is "major", the smaller "minor". Fourths, fifths and octaves come in one size so pure the old theorists called them "perfect". You don't need the full taxonomy yet; your ear is collecting the sounds, which is the part that can't be crammed later. The complete tour — all thirteen intervals with quality names — is in intervals . Go deeper: intervals as shapes → · intervals → Apply it: hum first, then play The shapes only pay off when the sound arrives before the finger. Reverse the flow: play the root, hum where you think the interval lands, then play the second note

t: Practice: The shape tour from A
b: All six pairs played clean from the same A root, and you can say each shape's name and personality before its second note sounds.
For fourths, fifths and octaves: your hummed guess lands on (or a hair from) the real note at least four times out of five.

Session 10 · Phase 2 — Fretboard & rhythm One finger per fret So far your left hand has been improvising its fingering. Today it gets a system — four fingers, four frets, one job each — plus the two little moves (hammer and pull) that let notes sing without a pluck. Tune up Warm-up: the shape tour, abridged From A: fourth, fifth, octave — say the names, hear the personalities. ▶ Hear it New thing: the spider Park your hand so your index hovers over fret 5 — then middle owns 6, ring owns 7, pinky owns 8. That's the whole system: one finger per fret , nobody moonlights. Crawl it slowly across all four strings. Why that worked A four-fret window with a finger assigned to each fret is a position — named after the index finger's fret, so you just played "5th position". Inside a position, every note is one finger-drop away: no searching, no stretching, no wasted motion. Most bass lines live inside one position for bars at a time; when the music leaves the window, you don't reach — you shift , sliding the whole frame as one unit ( shifting ). Here's the payoff — the Session 7 climb, written twice: once around open position, once entirely inside 5th position. Same notes, two homes: ▶ Hear it Numbers under the notes are fingers (1 = index … 4 = pinky). In the second pass A sits under 1, C under 4 on the same string, D under 1 on the next — one frame, zero travel. The quiet half of technique: muting Great bassists are defined less by the notes they start than the ones they stop. Fingers that aren't fretting rest lightly on the strings; the right thumb quiets the E string when you play higher — both hands share the silence work. It develops naturally if you listen for ring-over; the full checklist is in the left hand . Go deeper: positions & fingering → · the left hand → Apply it: hammer-ons and pull-offs Two notes, one pluck: pluck A (index, fret 5) and hammer the ring finger down hard onto fret 7 — the B sounds by impact alone. Reverse it: with both fingers down, pluck, then
Frets 5-6-7-8 on every string with the assigned fingers only — each note clean, hand frame still, thumb loose behind the neck.
The hammered and pulled notes speak as loudly as the plucked ones — one pluck per pair, no volume drop on the second note.

t: Session 11 · The major scale under your hand
h: New thing: the G major box
b: Session 11 · Phase 3 — Theory in your hands The major scale under your hand Do-re-mi lives in a two-and-a-half-fret box, and your hand already has the finger system to play it. Learn the box once and you own it in every key — that's today's whole trick. Tune up Warm-up: the spider, briefly One trip up frets 2-3-4-5 on the E and A strings — the box you're about to learn lives right there. ▶ Hear it New thing: the G major box Start on G (E string, fret 3, middle finger ). Follow the tab exactly — the fingering under each note is the pattern your hand will keep forever. Go up, come back down, and sing the numbers "1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8" as you go. Yes, out loud; you know the drill. Why that worked You just played the major scale — the do-re-mi your ear has known since childhood. Its recipe is a fixed chain of whole and half steps: W-W-H-W-W-W-H , which on bass is just frets: +2 +2 +1 +2 +2 +2 +1 . Start anywhere, follow the recipe, get sunshine. The numbers you sang are degrees — they're how musicians name notes relative to home , and they're about to become your main language: "play the 5" works in every key, "play D" only works in one. The whole neck in G major — your one-octave box is the bottom corner of this map. Roots are solid; tap around, nothing here sounds wrong together. The two half steps are the flavor Only two neighbors sit one fret apart: 3→4 and 7→8. Those two "leaners" are where the scale's pull lives — hang on 7 and feel it ache toward 8. Every melody trick and every mode you'll ever meet comes down to where those half steps land; the full story is in the major scale . Go deeper: the major scale → Apply it: move the box The shape is movable — that's the superpower. Slide the whole pattern up two frets and it's A major . Start it from fret 8 and it's C major . Same fingers, same recipe, new key. Test yourself: hum "do" on any random fret, play the box from it, and hear do-re-mi appear under your hand. Quick check Wrap up One box, one recipe, every key. And y

t: Practice: The G major box, degrees sung
b: Up and down without pausing, every note clean, singing "1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8" in tune with what you play.

t: Practice: Same box, new homes: A and C
b: The identical fingering launched from fret 5 (A major) and fret 8 (C major) — no re-thinking, just relocation, do-re-mi intact both places.

h: New thing: move up a fifth, listen for the click
Apply it: walk the circle on the neck
b: Session 12 · Phase 3 — Theory in your hands Keys and the circle Move yesterday's box up a fifth and exactly one note changes. Do that twelve times and you've walked through every key in music and back home — that walk has a name, and today you play it. Tune up Warm-up: the G box One octave of G major, up and down, degrees sung. It's the reference point for everything today. ▶ Hear it New thing: move up a fifth, listen for the click Play the G box. Now start the same shape from D (A string, fret 5) and listen hard to the 7th degree . In G major that note was C; in D major it's C♯ — one semitone, one single changed note, and everything else survives the move. Why that worked A key is a home note plus its major-scale family. Move home up a fifth and the recipe forces exactly one resident to sharpen (the new 7th). Move up another fifth, one more sharp. Arrange all twelve homes by fifths and you get the circle of fifths — music's subway map. Neighbors share six of seven notes; opposite sides barely speak. The key signature — that cluster of sharps or flats at the start of a staff — is just the changed residents listed once, so the page doesn't nag you note by note. Tap any key — majors outside, relative minors inside. Each tap shows the signature and plays the root. And the flat direction? Walk the circle the other way — down a fifth (or up a fourth, same thing) — and instead of gaining sharps you gain flats: F has one, B♭ two, E♭ three… Bass players live on this side of the circle more than most, because horns love flat keys. Deep dive with the interactive signature builder in keys & the circle of fifths . Go deeper: keys & the circle of fifths → Apply it: walk the circle on the neck Here's the beautiful part: "up a fifth" is a shape you already own — next string, two frets up (Session 9). So the circle isn't abstract: it's a physical walk your hand can do. Say each root as you land. C–G–D–A–E one way, C–F–B♭–E♭ the other — those eight cover ninety percent of the songs

t: Practice: G box, D box — spot the changed note
b: Both scales fluent, and you can point at the one note that changed (C→C♯) and hear the difference it makes on the 7th degree's lean.
Roots around both sides of the circle from memory — sharps side C-G-D-A-E, flats side C-F-B♭-E♭ — each move played as its fifth/fourth shape, names said aloud.

h: New thing: the A minor box
Apply it: flip a song's mood
b: Session 13 · Phase 3 — Theory in your hands Minor: the other home Every major key hides a moodier twin inside it — same seven notes, different gravity. Today you play both twins, flip a song's mood with one fret, and find out your Session 1 riff was minor all along. Tune up Warm-up: the circle walk Sharp side up, flat side back — root names out loud. ▶ Hear it New thing: the A minor box Start on A (E string, fret 5, index this time) and follow the tab. Every note in this scale also lives in C major — yet it doesn't sound like sunshine at all, because home has moved to A. Why that worked That was the natural minor scale — recipe W-H-W-W-H-W-W (frets +2 +1 +2 +2 +1 +2 +2). A minor and C major hold identical notes; each is the other's relative . What changed is gravity : phrases now rest on A, and the little 3rd degree — C, a minor third above home, your "sad" shape from Session 9 — colors everything. Different question, different twin: keep the same home and just swap the scale's mood — A major versus A minor — and that pair is called parallel . Relative = same notes, new home. Parallel = same home, new notes. Three minors in one breath Natural minor is the raw material. Harmonic minor raises the 7th one fret so it leans home hard (instant Bond-villain flavor — you'll taste it in the exercise). Melodic minor smooths the resulting gap for rising melodies. Bassists mostly live in natural minor; knowing why the other two exist is enough for now — the full story is in minor scales . Go deeper: minor scales → Apply it: flip a song's mood The fastest ear-training on bass: change one note — the third — and flip a line from major to minor. Then taste harmonic minor's raised 7th. And yes: E–G–A, your day-one riff? That's the spine of E minor . You've been a minor-key player since Session 1. Quick check Wrap up Two homes, two twins (relative and parallel), and a one-fret mood switch under your fingers. Your ear now files every song it hears into one of two boxes — that's a supe
One octave of A natural minor up and down, degrees sung ("1 2 ♭3 4 5 ♭6 ♭7 8"), with the sad third landing dead in tune.

t: Practice: One fret flips the mood
b: The major and minor versions of the same line played back to back until the flip is obvious before it happens — then the harmonic-minor bar with its raised 7th leaning hard on home.

h: New thing: the E minor pentatonic box — free with your bass
b: Session 14 · Phase 3 — Theory in your hands Pentatonics: five safe notes Subtract the two touchiest notes from a scale and what's left can't play a wrong note. Today you learn the box every rock and funk bassist lives in — and then you improvise for the first time. Yes, you. Tune up Warm-up: the mood flip A major climb, A minor climb, and the harmonic-minor lean — ears first, then fingers. ▶ Hear it New thing: the E minor pentatonic box — free with your bass Five notes: E · G · A · B · D . On this instrument they practically play themselves — two open strings, three easy frets, no stretch anywhere. Learn it up and down until your hand stops thinking. Why that worked The pentatonic scale is a seven-note scale with the two leaners removed — the half-step neighbors that demand careful handling. E natural minor drops its 2nd and ♭6th, leaving 1-♭3-4-5-♭7: five notes with no half step anywhere , so nothing clashes hard against the chord. That's why it feels safe — not dumbed down, just de-mined. The box you played is the open-position corner of this map. Same five notes everywhere — the dots are your playground. The blue note Add one forbidden fruit — the ♭5, B♭ in E's case — and minor pentatonic becomes the blues scale . It's a passing sting, not a parking spot: slide through it on the way from A to B and you'll hear fifty years of blues in one fret. More (plus major pentatonic, its sunny cousin) in pentatonics & blues . Go deeper: pentatonics & the blues scale → Apply it: your first improvisation Time to make something up. Start the band, stay inside the five notes, and follow the three rules on the card. There are no wrong notes in the box — only phrases you haven't finished yet. Your backing band: groove machine — E vamp, rock 8ths, 90 bpm . Keep it running and come back to this card. Quick check Wrap up Five safe notes, one sting, and your first improvised phrases over a groove. The box will be your safety net for the rest of your playing life — and it cost you one

t: Practice: The open E minor pentatonic box
b: Up and down at 90 without looking — E G A B D E flowing as one gesture, open strings ringing as long as the fretted notes.

t: Practice: First improv: three rules, five notes
b: Two full minutes of made-up phrases over the vamp without stopping — phrases that breathe (play a bit, rest a bit) and land on E more often than not.

t: Session 15 · Triads: chords one note at a time
h: New thing: major, minor, diminished — three arpeggio shapes
Apply it: upgrade the three-chord song
b: Session 15 · Phase 3 — Theory in your hands Triads: chords one note at a time A chord is three notes stacked; a bassist plays them one at a time and calls it an arpeggio. Learn three small shapes today and your root-only lines suddenly get a vocabulary. Tune up Warm-up: the pentatonic box E minor pentatonic up and down, eyes closed by the third loop. ▶ Hear it New thing: major, minor, diminished — three arpeggio shapes All from the same A root. Major: root, third up one string one fret back, fifth up one string two frets on — the happy stack. Minor: same but the third drops a fret. Diminished: minor with the fifth squeezed down a fret too. Play each slowly and name what you hear: open, sad, clenched. Why that worked A triad is degrees 1-3-5 of a scale sounded together; play them in sequence and it's an arpeggio . The third sets the mood (major vs minor — you knew that already), the fifth adds stability, and the root names the whole thing. Those three notes are the chord tones — the notes that can't be wrong while their chord is sounding, which makes them a bassist's ammunition, ranked: root strongest, fifth safest, third most colorful . Why diminished sounds "clenched" Stack two minor thirds and the outer interval is a tritone — six semitones, the most restless sound in the system. Diminished triads live on the major scale's 7th degree, wanting resolution. You'll rarely park on one, but you'll pass through them — details in triads . Go deeper: triads → · roots & chord tones → Apply it: upgrade the three-chord song Session 4's E–A–D song, three energy levels: roots only (you own this), then root–fifth (the country/rock workhorse), then the full root–third–fifth climb. Feel your line get richer while the job stays the same. Root–fifth secret: the fifth also lives below the root — same fret, one string down. Low fifths sound huge. Quick check Wrap up Three shapes, a ranked ammunition list, and a bass line that grew from one note to three without losing its job. This is
Major, minor and diminished arpeggios from A, up and back, each shape's sound nameable with eyes closed: open, sad, clenched.

t: Practice: The three-chord song, three energy levels
b: E–A–D–A played three ways without stopping — roots, root-fifth, root-third-fifth — changes still landing dead on beat 1 at every level.

t: Session 16 · Seventh chords and chord symbols
h: New thing: the four seventh flavors
Apply it: play straight from symbols
b: Session 16 · Phase 3 — Theory in your hands Seventh chords and chord symbols Add one more note to the triad stack and chords learn to yearn, float and strut. Today you play the four flavors every chart is written in — and start reading "Am7" and "G7" like short sentences. Tune up Warm-up: triads, three moods A major, A minor, A diminished arpeggios — say the moods as you play. ▶ Hear it New thing: the four seventh flavors Same A root, four stacks — each one is a triad plus one more note on top. Play each arpeggio slowly and give the flavor a word of your own: dreamy? bluesy? mellow? anxious? The words you pick will stick better than anyone else's. Why that worked A seventh chord is 1-3-5 plus the 7th — and the four combinations of major/minor third with major/minor seventh give the four flavors: maj7 (major 3rd + major 7th — dreamy, at rest), 7 ("dominant" — major 3rd + ♭7 — bluesy, pulling somewhere), m7 (minor 3rd + ♭7 — mellow, neutral), and m7♭5 (everything lowered — anxious, wants out). And the symbols are grammar, not code: the capital letter is the root , then modifiers read left to right — "m" makes the third minor, "7" adds the flat seventh, "maj7" adds the major one. C7 and Cmaj7 are different chords ; the word "maj" belongs to the seventh, not the triad. That one sentence prevents years of confusion. What the bassist actually plays over sevenths Rarely the whole stack. The workhorse is still root and fifth; the ♭7 is the first color worth adding (root–5–♭7 outlines a dominant chord unmistakably); the third stays the mood-teller. Where each flavor lives inside a key — and why the V chord is always a dominant 7 — is next session's story, with the full tour in seventh chords . Go deeper: seventh chords & symbols → Apply it: play straight from symbols A real chart moment: four symbols, no tab until you check. Read each symbol out loud in plain words first ("C major seven… A minor seven…"), find the root, then walk the arpeggio the chart implies. This progress

t: Practice: Four flavors from one root
b: All four seventh arpeggios from A played clean, and you can name the flavor from the first three notes with your eyes shut.

t: Practice: The chart drill: Cmaj7 · Am7 · Dm7 · G7
b: Three passes from the symbols alone — roots, then root+7th, then full arpeggios — each chord spoken in plain words before its bar arrives.

h: New thing: the one, the four, the five
Apply it: the famous three, by numbers
b: Session 17 · Phase 4 — Grooves & lines Numbers for progressions "It's a one-four-five in G" — and every musician in the room knows the whole song. Today you learn the number language, play the three most famous progressions in music, and feel why the V chord always wants to go home. Tune up Warm-up: the chart drill Cmaj7 · Am7 · Dm7 · G7 roots, symbols spoken aloud. ▶ Hear it New thing: the one, the four, the five Number the G major scale's notes 1-7. Build a chord on each and number those too. Now play the progression I–IV–V–I in G: roots G, C, D, G. Congratulations — you just played the skeleton of a few thousand songs. Why that worked Every major key grows the same seven-chord family from its scale — same qualities in the same slots, every key. So musicians name chords by slot number : Roman numerals in writing (uppercase major: I, IV, V; lowercase minor: ii, iii, vi; the odd one out vii°), or spoken Nashville numbers ("one, four, five"). Learn a song as numbers and you know it in all twelve keys at once. And the pull you feel from V back to I? The V chord contains the key's 7th degree — the leaner — aching that last half step home. That ache is called a cadence , and it's the engine every chorus runs on. The family, spelled out in G I=G · ii=Am · iii=Bm · IV=C · V=D · vi=Em · vii°=F♯dim. Same pattern in every major key — swap the roots, keep the qualities. Minor keys have their own family (i, iv, v get moody). The full table, plus why this buys you instant jam-session literacy, is in diatonic harmony . Go deeper: diatonic harmony → Apply it: the famous three, by numbers Three progressions cover most of the radio: I–V–vi–IV (the pop machine), I–vi–IV–V (the doo-wop), ii–V–I (the jazz handshake). Play each in G from the numbers, then prove the point: play one of them in C without any new learning. Then with the band: I–V–vi–IV in G · doo-wop · ii–V–I . Quick check Wrap up You speak numbers now — the language that makes every key the same key and every jam session

t: Practice: I–IV–V–I in G, roots and fifths
b: The progression played from the numbers — spoken "one… four… five… one" as the bars turn — first as roots, then root-fifth, changes glued to beat 1.

t: Practice: The famous three, then transposed
b: I–V–vi–IV, I–vi–IV–V and ii–V–I each played fluently in G — then I–V–vi–IV again in C with zero hesitation, proving the numbers transfer.

t: Session 18 · Tone is in your hands
h: New thing: one line, three plucking spots
b: Session 18 · Phase 4 — Grooves & lines Tone is in your hands Before you ever touch an amp knob, your two hands hold five tone controls. Today you play one simple line many ways and discover you've owned a mixing desk since Session 1. Tune up Warm-up: I–IV–V by numbers In G, roots and fifths, numbers spoken. ▶ Hear it New thing: one line, three plucking spots Play the root–fifth line on the card three times: plucking over the end of the neck (fat, round, dub-like), in the middle (your everyday sound), and right by the bridge (tight, punchy, growly). Move nothing but your plucking hand's anchor point. Why that worked Near the bridge the string barely swings, so plucking there emphasizes its tight, trebly partials; over the neck it swings widest and blooms warm and deep. That's built-in EQ, no knobs. The other hand-controls: how hard you pluck (harder = brighter, not just louder), note length (staccato thump vs singing legato — the biggest one nobody notices), where you fret , and which string you choose for the same pitch (A on the E string ≠ A on the open A). Dynamics — loud and soft — are relative : a groove only sounds big because something before it was small. And an accent is one note leaning louder, never faster. OK, and the amp? Amp EQ is the same idea with electricity: bass knob = neck-ish warmth, treble = bridge-ish snap. Set it flat, get your hands making the sounds first, then let the amp exaggerate what your hands already do. The full tour (plus palm muting and pick tone) is in tone & dynamics . Go deeper: tone & dynamics → · the right hand, complete → Apply it: the dynamics ladder Same line, four intensities — whisper, speak, project, shout — then accents: everything medium except beat 1 leaning out. If a neighbor could hear only your dynamics, they should still know where bar lines are. Quick check Wrap up Five tone controls, all in your hands, all free. From now on, every exercise on this site is secretly also a tone exercise — because now you're listen

t: Practice: One line, three plucking spots
b: The same A root-fifth line sounding like three different basses — neck fat, middle even, bridge punchy — with volume staying constant across all three.

t: Practice: The dynamics ladder + the leaning one
b: Four clean intensity levels on demand, and an accent pattern where beat 1 audibly leads without speeding up — recorded or witnessed, the bar lines are hearable from dynamics alone.

Session 19 · Phase 4 — Grooves & lines The groove lab Time is the bassist's real instrument — the strings are just how you touch it. Today: three metronome games that build professional time, and the practice method that makes twenty good minutes beat two vague hours. Tune up Warm-up: the accent line Root-fifth on A with beat 1 leaning — yesterday's ladder, one pass. ▶ Hear it New thing: three click games The metronome stops being a ruler and becomes a sparring partner. Game 1 you know (click = every beat). Game 2 — backbeat : halve the tempo and hear each click as beats 2 and 4 , like a drummer's snare. Game 3 — half time : one click per bar, you carry the other three beats alone. Why that worked Fewer clicks = more of the timekeeping is yours . The wobble you felt in game 3 is exactly the skill being built: internal time . And the pros' vocabulary for micro-placement — playing a hair ahead (eager, punk), behind (fat, soulful), or on top (neutral, tight) of the beat — describes feels , not errors: chosen, consistent placement is groove; wandering placement is just late. The other half of today: how to practice anything . The method is an index card: one goal per session, written down. Slow enough to be perfect. Chunk the hard two beats, loop just them. Raise the tempo only when it's clean twice in a row. That's the entire secret; everything else is consistency. The 20–30 minute session shape 2 min tune + wander → 5 min warm-up/technique → 15 min the ONE thing on the card → 5 min play something fun so the session ends in music. Plateaus are normal (skills consolidate invisibly); practicing ≠ playing (both matter, know which you're doing). The full method: how to practice . Go deeper: timing & groove → · how to practice → Apply it: build your routine You've logged exercises for eighteen sessions — time they organized themselves. Open the routine builder : it reads your practice log and assembles a daily session from what's due. Build one now, save it, and let it be t

t: Practice: Three click games on one groove
b: The A root-fifth groove solid through all three games — full click, clicks as 2&4, one click per bar — without the wheels coming off at the hand-offs.
Two minutes locked with the groove machine at 90, then two more at 60 with the same confidence — slow exposing nothing the fast pass was hiding.

Session 20 · Phase 4 — Grooves & lines Approach notes The difference between a bassist who plays the changes and one who announces them is usually one note — placed just before the change, one fret away from it. Today you learn the announcement. Tune up Warm-up: pocket check One minute of root eighths on A at 84, placement chosen. ▶ Hear it New thing: hear the announcement Two versions of the same two-chord vamp. Version A: plain roots. Version B: identical, except the last beat before each change plays a note one fret below the next root. Listen to how version B makes the change sound planned . Why that worked That leaning note is an approach note : tension placed on a weak beat, resolved on the strong one. One fret below (or above) the target is a chromatic approach — it works from anywhere to anywhere, no theory required, because the half-step pull you've been meeting since Session 11 does all the work. The other two flavors: scale approaches (walk down the key's ladder into the target) and dominant approaches (jump to the fifth above, then land). The golden rule: the approach lives on the weak last beat; the target owns beat 1. Announce, then arrive — never the reverse. The enclosure — approach from both sides Jazz's favorite garnish: surround the target — one fret above, one below, then land (or scale-above, chromatic-below). Two announcements make the arrival feel inevitable. Double-chromatic runs (two frets out, sliding in) live in approach notes with played examples of every flavor. Go deeper: approach notes → Apply it: announce every change The I–V–vi–IV in G, with an approach into every chord — mix from-below and from-above until each seam has a favorite. This is the exercise that upgrades every song you already know. Band practice: I–V–vi–IV in G, 96 bpm — the machine plays plain roots; you play the version with announcements. Hear the difference you're adding. Quick check Wrap up Tension on the weak beat, arrival on the strong one — you now own the move
Both versions of the E–A vamp on demand — and in the announced one, the approach never steals beat 1: tension on "4", target dead on "1".
The full progression looping with a deliberate approach into all four chords — mixed directions, no approach ever louder or longer than its target.

h: Apply it: walk your own line
b: Session 21 · Phase 4 — Grooves & lines Walking bass 101 Four quarter notes a bar, never stopping, each bar a little road from this chord to the next. Walking sounds like jazz wizardry; it's actually a formula — and you already own every ingredient. Tune up Warm-up: announcements The E–A vamp with chromatic approaches on beat 4 — walking's seed. ▶ Hear it New thing: the four-beat formula Each bar: beat 1 — root. Beat 2 — a chord tone. Beat 3 — a scale note. Beat 4 — an approach into the next root. That's walking. Here's a full written chorus of the jazz handshake (Dm7 · G7 · Cmaj7) to steal, formula labeled under every note. Why that worked Walking is the whole course in four beats: strong beats get strong notes (root on 1, chord tone on 2), lighter beats get connective tissue (scale on 3), and beat 4 does Session 20's job — announcing the next arrival. The commitment is rhythmic: quarter notes never stop . No gaps, no doubling up; the unbroken chain IS the style. Notice you made choices: which chord tone, which scale note, up or down. Different choices, different road, same formula — that's why two bassists never walk the same line and both are right. Making it swing (a preview) Real jazz walking leans its quarters with a triplet undertow and occasionally cracks a beat into a skip. That's feel, and it arrives in Session 29 with the style itself. Today is about the note-choosing machine; the classic bar-shapes and the ii–V squeeze live in walking bass 101 . Go deeper: walking bass 101 → Apply it: walk your own line Same changes, your road. Use the formula each bar and make at least one different choice per bar from the written chorus. Then stretch to the doo-wop progression — four chords, same machine. Chart + click: ii–V–I in C, metronome only — the machine shows the chart and clicks; the walking is all yours. Quick check Wrap up Root, chord tone, scale, approach — you can now walk any progression you can name, which after Session 17 is all of them. The mystique was

t: Practice: A written chorus to steal (Dm7 · G7 · C)
b: The written walk memorized and unbroken at 80 — quarters never stopping, the R/tone/scale/approach roles nameable for every note you play.

t: Practice: Your own walk: same changes, your road
b: Four unbroken choruses of Dm7–G7–C with at least one choice per bar differing from the written line — then the doo-wop (C–Am–F–G) walked cold.

h: New thing: the map, roots only
Apply it: the line every bassist owns
b: Session 22 · Phase 4 — Grooves & lines The 12-bar blues If music has one shared campfire, it's twelve bars of blues. Learn the map today and you can walk into any jam on the planet, ask "blues in A?", and belong. Tune up Warm-up: walk the handshake One chorus of Dm7 · G7 · C from memory — roles said aloud. ▶ Hear it New thing: the map, roots only Twelve bars, three chords, in A: four bars of I (A7), two of IV (D7), two of I , then the turn: V (E7), IV, I, V . Play roots through the whole form and count the bars out loud — knowing where you are is the entire skill. Why that worked The 12-bar blues is a form — a repeating floor plan everyone memorizes so nobody needs a chart. Its grammar in numbers: I-I-I-I · IV-IV-I-I · V-IV-I-V. Every chord is a dominant 7th (that bluesy lean from Session 16 — in blues, tension never fully resolves, and that's the sound). The final V is the turnaround : it spins the wheel back to bar 1. The quick change Common variant: bar 2 jumps early to IV, back to I for bars 3-4 — the "quick change". At a jam someone just says "quick change" and everyone adjusts one bar. Turnaround vocabulary (that last V dressed up with approaches) and more variants: the 12-bar blues . Go deeper: the 12-bar blues → Apply it: the line every bassist owns The boogie line — root, 3, 5, 6 on each chord — older than electricity and still on the radio. Learn it on A7, move the same shape to D7 and E7 (it's movable, of course), then play the whole form. The band's waiting: 12-bar in A, shuffle, 96 — play the boogie line against it and count the bars. Lose your place? Keep grooving on the root till bar 1 comes round; that's a real-jam skill too. Quick check Wrap up Twelve bars, three chords, one movable line — and the map lives in your count now, not on paper. You're jam-ready in any key that starts with "blues in…". Next time: zoom out — whole songs. Verse-chorus, AABA, and reading the shorthand charts working musicians actually use. Mark Session 22 done

t: Practice: Roots through the form, bars counted
b: Three full choruses of roots in A — bar numbers spoken ("one, two… five… nine…") — without ever losing the map, even when your mouth disagrees with your hands.

t: Practice: The boogie line: R-3-5-6, movable
b: The R-3-5-6 shape fluent on A7, D7 and E7, then one full 12-bar chorus with the line — shape shifts landing on every seam without a stumble.

t: Session 23 · Forms and charts: play a whole song
h: New thing: read a real-world chart
b: Session 23 · Phase 4 — Grooves & lines Forms and charts: play a whole song Songs are buildings: verses, choruses, a bridge if the architect was feeling fancy. Today you read the shorthand working musicians actually get handed — and play two complete songs end to end. Tune up Warm-up: one blues chorus Twelve bars in A, boogie line, bars counted. Still in the body? Good. ▶ Hear it New thing: read a real-world chart Here's a song the way a bandleader would text it to you — Nashville numbers, key of C, one cell per bar: KEY: C (1=C 4=F 5=G 6-=Am) VERSE | 1 | 6- | 4 | 5 | (play twice) CHORUS | 4 | 5 | 1 | 6- | | 4 | 5 | 1 | 1 | FORM: Verse ×2 → Chorus → Verse → Chorus ×2 Decode it out loud first ("one is C, six-minus is A minor…"), then play the whole form, roots only, counting bars. Why that worked A chart compresses a song to what a rhythm section needs: the key, the bars, the changes, the form . Numbers instead of letters (Session 17 cashing in), a minus or dash for minor, one cell per bar. The two floor plans that cover most of popular music: verse–chorus (what you just played) and AABA — two verses, a contrasting bridge, verse home — the classic 32-bar shape of the standards. The bassist's job at the section seams : announce them. A fill, an approach, or just a firm root — the band changes rooms and you're the one opening the door. Repeats, endings, and staying found Real charts add repeat signs, first/second endings ("1.___ 2.___"), D.S. jumps and codas — traffic signs, all learnable in an evening. The deeper skill is chunking : memorize sections as units ("verse = 1 6- 4 5"), then just remember the order. Full chart grammar with examples: song forms & charts . Go deeper: song forms & charts → Apply it: an AABA standard The other great floor plan, in G — eight bars per section: KEY: G (1=G 2-=Am 4=C 5=D 6-=Em) A | 1 | 6- | 2- | 5 | (×2 — that's A A) B | 4 | 4 | 1 | 1 | | 2-| 2- | 5 | 5 | ← the bridge "goes somewhere" A | 1 | 6- | 2- | 5 | Play it root-fifth, and ma

t: Practice: The verse-chorus song, off the chart
b: The full form — verse ×2, chorus, verse, chorus ×2 — played from the text chart without stopping, place never lost, changes on every beat 1.
All 32 bars — A, A, bridge, A — in root-fifth with each section's first downbeat firmly announced, and you can say which section you're in at any moment.

How high or low a sound is. The perceived highness or lowness of a sound, set by its vibration frequency — faster vibration sounds higher. On bass, pressing closer to the body raises the pitch; thicker strings sit lower.
One named musical sound — like A, C♯, or E♭. A pitch with a name. Music uses twelve note names that repeat as you go higher or lower: A, A♯/B♭, B, C, C♯/D♭, D, D♯/E♭, E, F, F♯/G♭, G, G♯/A♭.

The four steel wires you pluck; each is named after the note it plays open. A 4-string bass in standard tuning has strings E–A–D–G, from thickest (lowest) to thinnest (highest). "The A string" means the second-thickest string, which sounds the note A when played open.
Playing a string without pressing any fret. A string plucked while no finger holds it down anywhere. Open strings sound each string's tuned note (E, A, D, or G) and are written as fret 0 in tab.

The metal strips along the neck; each one raises the note by one small step. The metal bars set into the fingerboard. Pressing a string just behind a fret shortens its vibrating length; each fret raises the pitch by one semitone (half step). "5th fret" means the space just behind the fifth metal bar counting from the headstock.

The same note, higher or lower — like two people singing the same tune, one deeper. The distance between a note and the next note with the same name (12 semitones). Notes an octave apart vibrate in a 2:1 ratio and sound like "the same note, higher/lower". On bass: two strings up and two frets over.

One fret's worth of distance — the smallest step in Western music. The smallest interval in common Western music: the distance from one fret to the next on the same string. Twelve semitones make an octave.
Two frets' worth of distance. An interval of two semitones — two frets on the same string. Scales are usually described as patterns of whole and half steps.

The distance between two notes. The gap in pitch between two notes, counted in scale steps and given names like "perfect fifth" or "minor third". Intervals are the units bass lines are built from — on the fretboard each interval is a repeatable shape.

The home note a chord or scale is built on — the note that gives it its name. The foundational note of a chord or scale: the C in C major, the A in Am7. The bassist's first job in most music is to sound the root when the chord changes.

A set of notes that belong together, played in order — the palette a song draws from. An ordered collection of notes spanning an octave, defined by its pattern of whole and half steps (e.g. the major scale: W-W-H-W-W-W-H). Melodies and bass lines are mostly built from a scale's notes.

The do-re-mi scale — the sunny-sounding yardstick everything else is measured against. A seven-note scale built W-W-H-W-W-W-H from its root. It defines the key, names the scale degrees (1–7), and is the reference for describing every chord and interval ("a minor third is a flattened 3").

The moodier cousin of the major scale. Usually means the natural minor scale: W-H-W-W-H-W-W, equivalent to a major scale started from its 6th degree. Variants (harmonic, melodic) alter the 6th and 7th notes for stronger pull back to the root.

Two keys that share the exact same notes — one feels bright, one feels dark. Every major key has a minor key with the identical notes and key signature, rooted a minor third below it (C major ↔ A minor). Which one you're "in" depends on which note feels like home.

A five-note scale that's almost impossible to make sound wrong. A scale with five notes per octave. The minor pentatonic (1 ♭3 4 5 ♭7) and major pentatonic (1 2 3 5 6) drop the clash-prone notes of their parent scales, which is why they're the workhorse of rock, blues, and funk lines.

The minor pentatonic plus one deliciously wrong note. The minor pentatonic with an added ♭5 (1 ♭3 4 ♭5 5 ♭7). That extra note — the "blue note" — is used as a passing sting between 4 and 5 rather than a resting place.

The same seven notes, but a different one feels like home. A scale made by treating a different degree of a parent scale as the root. The major scale's seven modes (Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian) each have a distinct flavor; Dorian and Mixolydian matter most to bassists.

A note's house number inside its scale — 1 through 7. The position of a note within a scale, counted from the root: in C major, C=1, D=2, E=3… Degrees let one description work in every key ("play root and 5" works in C, in F♯, anywhere).

Home base — the note (or chord) where the music feels settled. The first degree of the key and the chord built on it (the I chord). Music builds tension by leaving the tonic and resolves it by coming back.
