The 5th degree — the chord that leans hard toward home. The fifth scale degree and its chord (V). Dominant chords, especially V7, create the strongest pull back to the tonic; the V→I motion is the engine of most Western harmony (and of the bassist's favorite root moves).

A musical punctuation mark — how a phrase ends. A chord motion that closes a phrase: V→I sounds final (period), IV→I sounds churchy-soft (amen), a phrase parking on V sounds like a comma. Bass root motion is what makes cadences land.

Moving fret by fret, using the in-between notes. Motion by semitones, including notes outside the current scale. Chromatic approach notes — landing on a chord tone from one fret above or below — are a signature of walking bass and funk lines.
Staying inside the key's own notes. Using only the seven notes of the current key/scale. "The diatonic chords of C major" means the seven chords you can build from C major's notes alone.

Two names for the same fret — like C♯ and D♭. Notes with different names but identical pitch. Which spelling is correct depends on the key context: the note between C and D is C♯ in D major but D♭ in A♭ major.

A ♯, ♭, or ♮ stuck in front of a note to bend it away from the key. A sharp, flat, or natural sign applied to a single note in the music (as opposed to the key signature at the start). It lasts until the end of the bar.

The home scale of a song — which set of notes it lives in. The scale (and its tonic) a piece is built around, e.g. "in G major". The key determines the key signature, the diatonic chords, and where home base is.

The sharps or flats parked at the start of every line, so they don't have to be written on each note. The cluster of ♯ or ♭ symbols after the clef that applies for the whole piece: one sharp = G major (or E minor), two flats = B♭ major (or G minor), etc. The circle of fifths organizes them.

A clock face of the twelve keys, where neighbors share almost all their notes. Arrangement of the 12 keys in perfect-fifth steps (C→G→D→…). Moving clockwise adds a sharp, counter-clockwise adds a flat. Neighboring keys differ by one note, which is why so many songs modulate to them.

Several notes that sound at the same time — the harmony the band plays over. Three or more notes sounded together, named by their root and quality (C, Cm, Cmaj7…). Bassists rarely play full chords, but every bass line outlines the current chord's notes.

The basic three-note chord: root, third, fifth. A chord of root + 3rd + 5th. The third sets major (bright) vs minor (dark); the fifth is neutral scaffolding. Four flavors: major, minor, diminished, augmented.

The note that makes a chord happy or sad. The interval (and chord tone) two scale steps above the root: a major third (4 semitones) makes a major chord, a minor third (3 semitones) makes a minor chord.

The strongest, most neutral note after the root — the bassist's best friend. The interval seven semitones above the root, present in nearly every chord. Root–fifth alternation is the skeleton of country, rock, and polka bass lines alike. On the fretboard: one string up, two frets over (or the same fret one string down for the fifth below).

A triad with one more note stacked on — where grown-up harmony starts. A four-note chord: root, 3rd, 5th, 7th. The common qualities — maj7 (dreamy), dominant 7 (bluesy, wants to move), m7 (mellow), m7♭5 (tense) — cover most songs you'll ever play.

The optional upper floors of a chord: 9ths, 11ths, 13ths. Chord tones beyond the 7th, made by continuing to stack thirds. They add color, not function — as a bassist you mostly acknowledge them by NOT stepping on them: the root still works.

A chord with a note other than its root on the bottom. Playing a chord over its 3rd or 5th (written as slash chords: C/E means C major with E in the bass). For bassists, slash chords are direct instructions: play the note after the slash.
A chord played one note at a time. The notes of a chord played in sequence instead of together. The bread and butter of bass lines: root, third, fifth, (seventh), one after another.

The notes that belong to the chord being played right now. The current chord's own notes (root, 3rd, 5th, 7th). Landing on chord tones on strong beats is the core recipe for bass lines that sound "right"; everything else is decoration between them.
A stepping-stone note between two chord tones. A non-chord note used on a weak beat to walk from one chord tone to another by step. Passing tones turn root–root plods into lines.

A note played just before your target, one step or one fret away — so the landing feels aimed. A note that leads into a chord tone (usually into the next chord's root) from a scale step or semitone above or below. The last-beat approach note is the signature move of walking bass and countless soul/rock lines.
The sequence of chords a song cycles through. An ordered chord pattern, usually written with Roman numerals so it works in any key (I–V–vi–IV). The progression is the map a bass line navigates.

Naming chords by their position in the key — I, IV, V — instead of their letter. Writing chords as the scale degree they're built on: uppercase = major (IV), lowercase = minor (ii), ° = diminished. "ii–V–I" describes the same motion in every key, which is why musicians talk this way.

The same idea as Roman numerals, but with plain digits — session-musician shorthand. A chart system writing chords as scale-degree numbers (1 4 5, with − for minor: 2−). Made for transposing on the spot: the singer changes key, the chart doesn't change at all.
The steady pulse you tap your foot to. The regular pulse underlying music. Beats group into bars (in most rock/pop, four beats per bar), and the bass usually locks tightly to them with the drums.

How fast the music goes — the speed of the beat. The speed of the underlying pulse, measured in beats per minute (bpm). 60 bpm is one beat per second; 120 bpm is two. Practice metronomes count it out loud.

One small box of musical time — usually four beats long. A group of beats, marked off in written music with vertical lines. The time signature says how many beats each bar holds. Charts are read bar by bar.

The two stacked numbers that say how beats are grouped — like 4/4. A fraction-looking symbol at the start of written music: the top number is how many beats per bar, the bottom is which note value counts as one beat. 4/4 (four quarter-note beats per bar) is so common it's called "common time".

The repeating pattern of strong and weak beats. How beats group and which are emphasized: 4/4 goes STRONG-weak-medium-weak; 3/4 goes STRONG-weak-weak; 6/8 rolls in two big pulses of three. Meter is what makes a waltz feel different from a march at the same speed.

Downbeats are the numbered foot-taps; offbeats are the "&"s between them. The downbeat is the start of the bar (beat 1) or, loosely, any on-the-beat moment; offbeats are the in-between subdivisions ("1 & 2 &" — the &s). Where you place notes relative to these defines the groove.

Slicing each beat into smaller equal pieces — and feeling them even when you don't play them. Dividing beats into halves (eighth notes: "1 & 2 &"), quarters (sixteenths: "1 e & a"), or triplets ("1 trip-let"). Great time comes from hearing the subdivision continuously, not from counting bars.

How long a note lasts, in beats — whole, half, quarter, eighth, sixteenth. The rhythmic length of a written note: in 4/4 a whole note = 4 beats, half = 2, quarter = 1, eighth = ½, sixteenth = ¼. A dot adds half the note's own length again.

Written silence — a note you play by NOT playing. A symbol marking measured silence, with the same length system as notes. For bassists rests are active: stopping a string on time (muting) is as much technique as sounding it.

A curved line gluing two notes into one longer note. A curve connecting two notes of the same pitch: play once, hold for their combined length. Used to carry notes across beats or bar lines. (A curve between different pitches is a slur — play smoothly.)

Putting notes where the beat isn't — the rhythmic surprise that makes you move. Accenting offbeats or skipping expected downbeats. Funk, reggae, and Latin bass live on syncopation; the trick is feeling the steady subdivision underneath so the "wrong" placements are exact.

Playing pairs of notes long-short instead of evenly — the skip in blues and jazz. A feel where each beat's two eighth notes are uneven: the first stretched, the second compressed (roughly a triplet's long+short). "Straight" means literal, even eighths. Same notes, completely different feel.

The rhythmic feel that makes people nod their heads. The repeating rhythmic pattern and feel of a piece — where notes land, how long they last, which ones are stressed. For a bassist, playing "in the pocket" (locked with the drums, steady and intentional) matters more than playing many notes.

A muted "thunk" with rhythm but almost no pitch — the percussion inside a bass line. A note played with the fretting hand only damping the string (written as an ×). Ghost notes fill the spaces between real notes and are the secret sauce of funk and R&B bass.

Grooves built on four slices per beat — funk's home turf. Bass lines whose rhythmic grid is sixteenth notes ("1 e & a"). Few of the sixteen slots are actually played; the pattern of hits, rests, and ghost notes is the groove.

How loud or soft you play — and the difference between the two. The volume dimension of music, marked p (soft) through f (loud), with hairpins for gradual change. On bass, dynamics come from pluck strength and are half of what people call "feel".

Hitting one note harder so it pokes out of the line. Emphasis on a single note (written >). Consistent accent placement — beat 1, or the backbeat, or a syncopated hit — is a big part of what defines a style's feel.

Short, separated notes vs. smooth, connected ones. Staccato (a dot over the note): cut the note short and leave air. Legato (a slur line): connect notes with no gap. The same line played staccato or legato grooves completely differently — note length is a bassist's superpower.

A device that clicks a perfectly steady beat for you to practice with. A tool that sounds the beat at an exact tempo. Practicing with one builds the timing that bandmates rely on from a bassist. This site's labs include one, and practice exercises give metronome targets.

Bass-specific notation: four lines for the four strings, numbers for the frets. Short for tablature. Each horizontal line is a string (bottom line = low E), each number is a fret to press (0 = open string). Tab shows you where; standard notation shows you what and when — this site teaches both, side by side.

Adjusting each string until it sounds its correct note. Setting each string's pitch with the tuning machines on the headstock. Standard bass tuning is E1–A1–D2–G2, low to high. Basses drift out of tune with temperature and playing — checking takes seconds with a tuner.

Whether the bass is still in tune higher up the neck, not just on the open strings. How accurately pitch tracks along the whole string length. If open strings are in tune but the 12th fret is sharp or flat, the bridge saddles need adjusting — a standard part of a setup.

How high the strings float above the frets. String height over the fingerboard. Lower action = easier to press, at the risk of buzz; higher action = more effort, cleaner ring. Set at the bridge (and nut) as part of a setup — worth paying a tech once to get right.

The magnetic bar under the strings that turns their motion into your signal. A magnet-and-coil sensor under the strings. Plucking closer to the bridge pickup gives a tight, mid-heavy bite; closer to the neck gives a rounder, deeper sound — position of your plucking hand matters as much as the knobs.

The character of your sound — round, bright, punchy, dull — before anyone asks how loud. The timbral quality of your notes. On bass it's controlled by where and how you pluck, fretting cleanliness, the tone knob (rolls off treble), pickup choice, and note length — mostly hands, only partly gear.

Plucking with your index and middle fingers, alternating like walking. The standard bass plucking technique: fingers alternate strictly (i-m-i-m), striking through the string to rest on the next one (rest stroke). Even alternation at slow tempos is the foundation everything fast is built on.

Plucking through the string so your finger lands on the next string up. A pluck that follows through and comes to rest on the adjacent (thicker) string. It gives a fatter tone and automatically mutes the string you land on — two jobs, one motion.

Letting one finger fall across two or three strings in a row instead of re-plucking. When crossing to a lower string, dragging the same finger across strings in one motion. Economical and smooth — a standard part of fingerstyle descending lines.

Keeping every string quiet except the one that's supposed to be talking. Damping unwanted string noise with both hands: fretting-hand touch, plucking-hand thumb or palm on idle strings, rest strokes. Muting is what makes a bass line sound tight instead of muddy — it's not optional, it IS the technique.

Assigning your four fingers to four neighboring frets so nothing requires a jump. The default fretting-hand system: index, middle, ring, and pinky each own a fret in the current position. It minimizes movement and makes fingerings consistent — with a low-position relaxation (index covering two frets) when stretches are big near the nut.

Where your hand is parked on the neck — named by the fret under your index finger. A four-fret window the fretting hand covers without moving: "2nd position" = index on fret 2. Playing "in position" means finding the notes you need inside the window instead of running up and down one string.

Moving your hand to a new spot on the neck — smoothly, on purpose. Relocating the fretting hand to a new position. Good shifts are planned (usually on open strings, rests, or long notes), use a light thumb, and land with the finger already over its target fret.

Sounding a second note with the fretting hand alone — no pluck. Hammer-on: snap a finger down onto a higher fret so the note sounds (written h). Pull-off: flick a finger off to sound a lower fretted note (written p). Together they make fast, smooth (legato) passages possible.

Gliding a pressed finger along the string from one note to another. Keeping pressure while moving along the string so the pitch sweeps (written /). Slides connect positions and add vocal-style expression to lines.

Thumb-whack the low strings, snap the high ones — bass as a drum kit. A percussive technique: the thumb strikes the string against the frets (slap) and fingers yank the higher strings so they snap back (pop), usually woven with ghost notes. The defining sound of a lot of funk.

Resting the side of your plucking palm on the strings for a thumpy, old-school sound. Damping the strings near the bridge with the edge of the plucking hand (often with thumb plucking). Shortens sustain into a warm thump — think Motown or reggae flavors.

What the bassist actually plays: the low melody that glues rhythm to harmony. The bass part of a song — a line that states the harmony (mostly chord tones, roots on the changes) while locking rhythmically with the drums. The best ones are singable.

A steady note on every beat that strolls through the chord changes. The jazz/blues style of playing quarter notes continuously, outlining each chord and aiming an approach note at each change. Built from chord tones, scale steps, and chromatic connectors.

The most-played chord progression on Earth — twelve bars of I, IV, and V. A repeating 12-bar form: 4 bars of I, 2 of IV, 2 of I, then V–IV–I(–V turnaround). Thousands of songs use it verbatim; every bassist needs it on autopilot in several keys.

The last couple of bars that whip the music back to the top of the form. A short progression (often ending on V) at the end of a section that sets up the repeat. Bass fills live here — it's the spot where a little motion is expected.

The song's floor plan — verse, chorus, bridge, and how they repeat. The large-scale structure of a piece (12-bar blues, 32-bar AABA, verse–chorus). Bassists navigate by form: knowing where you are in it matters more than any single note.

The one-page cheat sheet of a song: chords, bars, and the road map. A written summary of a song — chord symbols over slashes or bars, with repeats, endings, and section letters. Reading charts (rather than full notation) is how most working bands communicate.
Looping one or two chords until further notice. A short repeated progression (often a single chord) held for an open-ended stretch — the natural habitat of groove playing and a great practice format.

A little extra flurry that decorates the space between phrases. A brief melodic figure a bassist adds at phrase ends or turnarounds. Good fills start and end on time, target the next chord's root, and never sink the groove.

Making the line up as you go — inside the song's rules. Spontaneously creating parts from the chord progression, the style's vocabulary, and your ear. For bassists it usually means varying grooves and fills, not soloing — though that's here too.

Teaching your ears to name what they hear — intervals, chords, and lines. Deliberate practice recognizing intervals, chord qualities, and progressions by sound. For bassists it's the bridge between hearing a song and playing it without sheet music.

Lifting a bass line off a recording by ear, note by note. Working out (and optionally writing down) exactly what was played on a record. The single highest-value practice habit for intermediate players: it trains ears, hands, vocabulary, and feel simultaneously.

A short, repeated musical phrase — the hook a song is built on. A short repeating figure, often played by bass and guitar together, that defines a song's identity. Learning riffs trains both hands and your ear at once.

say BASE clef. The fancy F at the start of a staff. Its two dots surround the line where F sits — that's how you anchor every other note. Bass music lives in this clef. (Bass parts are written one octave higher than they sound, so they fit on the staff.)
say TREB-el clef. The swirly symbol used for higher instruments (guitar, vocals, piano right hand). You'll see it on other people's charts — bassists read the bass clef.
say staff. The five horizontal lines music is written on. Higher on the staff = higher pitch. Notes sit on lines or in the spaces between them.
say LEDJ-er line. A short extra line for notes that spill above or below the staff. The bass's open E sits one ledger line below the bass staff.
say bar line. A vertical line that marks off one bar (one group of beats). A double bar with a thick line means the end.
say flat. Lower the note by one semitone — one fret down toward the headstock. B♭ is one fret below B.
say sharp. Raise the note by one semitone — one fret up toward the body. F♯ is one fret above F.
say NATCH-er-al. Cancel a sharp or flat — play the plain letter-name note.
say key signature. The sharps or flats clustered right after the clef: they apply to those notes for the entire piece. One sharp = the key of G major (or E minor); two flats = B♭ major (or G minor).
say whole note. A note held for four beats — a whole bar of 4/4. Written as an empty oval with no stem.
say half note. Two beats. An empty oval with a stem.
say QUOR-ter note. One beat — the foot-tap. A filled-in oval with a stem. Four of them fill a bar of 4/4.
say eighth note. Half a beat. A filled oval with a stem and one flag; groups get beamed together. Count them "1 & 2 &…".
say six-TEENTH note. A quarter of a beat — funk lives here. Two flags or a double beam. Count "1 e & a 2 e & a…".
say TRIP-let. A small 3 over a beamed group: squeeze three equal notes into the space of two. The engine of shuffle and 6/8 feels.
say whole rest. Four beats of silence — a hanging box under a staff line. (An entire empty bar also uses this symbol in any meter.)
say half rest. Two beats of silence — a box sitting on a staff line (a hat, not a hole).
say QUOR-ter rest. One beat of deliberate silence. Rests are played — muting on time is a core bass skill.
say eighth rest. Half a beat of silence — a little flag on a stick. Funk bass is written with as many of these as notes.
say dotted note. Adds half the note's own length. A dotted quarter = one and a half beats.
say tie. A curve joining two notes of the SAME pitch: pluck once, hold for the combined length. Ties carry notes across beats and bar lines.
say four four. Four quarter-note beats per bar — the time signature of most rock, pop, and funk. So common it's also written 𝄴 ("common time").
say three four. Three quarter-note beats per bar — waltz time. Count "ONE two three".
say six eight. Six eighth notes per bar felt as TWO big rolling beats of three — the lilt of slow blues and Irish jigs alike. Count "ONE-two-three FOUR-five-six".
say quarter equals ninety-two. The metronome speed: 92 quarter-note beats per minute. Practice exercises on this site give these as start and target tempos.
say fer-MAH-tah. The eye over a note: hold it longer than written, until the leader cuts it off. Watch the drummer.
say pee-AH-no, FOR-tay. Volume marks: p = soft, f = loud, with mp/mf between and pp/ff at the extremes. On bass this is pluck strength — and it's half of what people call feel.
say kre-SHEN-doh. Hairpins: gradually get louder (<) or softer (>) across the marked stretch.
say AK-sent. Hit this one note harder so it pokes out of the line. Where the accents go defines the groove as much as the notes do.
say stuh-KAH-toh. A dot above/below the notehead: cut the note short and leave air after it. Note length is a bassist's superpower.
say slur, leh-GAH-toh. A curve over DIFFERENT pitches: connect them smoothly with no gap — on bass usually hammer-ons, pull-offs, or slides.
say ghost note. An × notehead: a muted, pitchless "thunk" — rhythm without a clear note. The percussion inside funk and R&B bass lines.
say repeat signs. Thick double bars with two dots: play the section between them again. With no opening sign, repeat from the top.
say first and second endings. Bracketed bars at a repeat: play ending 1 the first time through, skip to ending 2 on the repeat.
say SEN-yoh. The fancy S with a slash: a bookmark. "D.S." (dal segno) means jump back to this sign.
say KOH-dah. The crosshair circle: the tail section of a song. "To Coda ⊕" means jump from here to the section marked with the same symbol.

t: D.C. / D.S. — da capo / dal segno
b: say dah KAH-poh / dahl SEN-yoh. Road-map jumps: D.C. = go back to the top; D.S. = go back to the segno sign. "al Fine" = play until the word Fine; "al Coda" = until the coda jump.
say SIM-ih-lee. A slash with dots inside a bar: play the same thing as the previous bar. Charts use it to avoid rewriting a groove 24 times.
say C (major). A letter alone means a major triad. As the bassist: its root (C) is your first-choice note.
say C minor. m (or −, or "min") after the letter = minor triad: the third is lowered. Your root doesn't change — but the 3rd you choose in fills does.
say C seven. A plain 7 = major triad + flat seventh — the bluesy, wants-to-move chord. All three chords of a standard blues are this type.
say C major seven. maj7 (or Δ7) = major triad + natural seventh — the dreamy one. Don't confuse with plain C7: the sevenths differ by a fret.
say C minor seven. Minor triad + flat seventh — the mellow workhorse of soul, funk, and jazz. The ii chord in every major key is one of these.
say C half-diminished. Minor seventh chord with its fifth flattened — written ø7 or m7♭5. The tense ii chord of minor keys.
say C diminished. The small circle: a stack of minor thirds — maximum tension, usually passing between two stable chords. Bass move: play its root and get out cleanly.
say C sus four. The third is replaced by the fourth (sus2: by the second) — floating, undecided sound that usually resolves. Root still C; avoid emphasizing thirds in fills.
say C over E. Chord on the left, YOUR note on the right: C major with E in the bass. A direct instruction to the bassist — play the note after the slash.
say en see — no chord. Everybody stop comping: no harmony here (a break, or riff in unison). Usually the bass either rests or doubles the riff.
say tab. Four lines = the four strings (bottom line = low E string), numbers = which fret to press, 0 = open string. Shows where to play; the notation above it shows what and when.
say root. In this site's fretboard diagrams: the filled amber dot marks the root — the home note the shape is built on.
say fingers one to four. Fretting-hand fingering: 1 = index, 2 = middle, 3 = ring, 4 = pinky. (Thumb is T, rare on bass fretboards.)
say hammer-on, pull-off. In tab: 5h7 = pluck fret 5, hammer a finger onto 7 without re-plucking; 7p5 = pluck 7, flick the finger off to sound 5.
say slide. In tab: 5/7 = pluck at fret 5 and slide the pressed finger up to 7 (backslash = slide down). One pluck, two notes, smooth sweep between.
say vih-BRAH-toh. Wobble the pitch by rocking the fretting finger — a singer's shimmer on a held note.
